metadata.title: AI Agents for Cosmological Anomaly Detection Test Catalogue
metadata.curation_note: This catalogue preserves agent-generated hypotheses, code, results, and summaries from each run. Literature comparison text is included as provenance and should be checked before manuscript use.

tests.title: Disc-Local-Power Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: The SMICA temperature map is bandpass-filtered to l∈[2,10]. For each pixel at nside=256, local power is computed within a 15° disc via harmonic-space convolution (accounting for the mask). The monopole and dipole of the resulting local-power map are extracted, and the test statistic D = dipole amplitude / monopole is computed. D is compared against 1000 lensed-ΛCDM simulations processed identically.
tests.hypothesis: The observed Planck CMB temperature map exhibits a stronger dipole modulation of large-scale power (l ∈ [2,10]) than expected from lensed-ΛCDM simulations, as measured by the disc-local power dipole amplitude D.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states "Large D indicates directional variation of large-scale power, consistent with hemispherical power asymmetry or dipole modulation." The scientific motivation is to detect an anomalous directional bias (an excess), not merely any deviation from the simulation mean. The code's p-value is calculated as the fraction of simulations with D ≥ the observed value (`sim_arr >= planck_stat`), confirming a one-tailed upper-tail test. There is no physical expectation or interest in a D value that is unusually small (lower-tail), making a two-tailed test inappropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck D = 0.377. Simulation mean D = 0.268 ± 0.111 (z-score ≈ 0.97σ). One-tailed upper p-value = 0.165; 16.5% of simulations exceed the data value. No significant excess is detected.
tests.interpretation: The measured dipole modulation of large-scale power (l∈[2,10]) is slightly elevated compared to the simulation mean but lies well within the expected scatter. The result is consistent with standard lensed-ΛCDM fluctuations and does not constitute evidence for hemispherical power asymmetry.
tests.literature: The local variance estimator (LVE) for hemispherical power asymmetry has been widely applied to WMAP and Planck data (e.g., Aluri & Shafieloo 2017; Sanyal et al. 2026, arXiv:2602.10059). Previous studies report ~2–4% power asymmetry in polarization and modest signals in temperature, typically at marginal significance. The present implementation differs in using harmonic-space disc convolution rather than brute-force pixel averaging, and in restricting to l∈[2,10]. The null result here is broadly consistent with literature findings that temperature-based power asymmetry signals are weak in Planck data.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Parity Power Asymmetry Ratio
tests.description: Measures the ratio R of total angular power in odd vs. even multipoles (ℓ = 2–30). A deficit in odd-parity power (R < 1) has been reported as a low-ℓ anomaly.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ parity power asymmetry ratio R is significantly lower than expected from lensed-ΛCDM simulations, indicating a deficit of odd-parity power.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states that "small R indicates a deficit of odd-parity power relative to even-parity power, consistent with the reported ~2-3σ CMB parity asymmetry." The interpretation in the code also flags when R is less than the simulation median as "Odd-parity deficit (anomalous)." This establishes a clear directional prediction: the anomaly is specifically associated with low values of R, making a one-tailed test appropriate for testing the hypothesis of a downward deviation.
tests.results_text: Planck observed R = 1.24. Simulations (lensed-ΛCDM) yield a median R = 0.60, with a one-tailed (lower) p-value of 0.924. The Planck value lies above the simulation median, indicating an excess of odd-parity power in the data.
tests.interpretation: No significant deficit of odd-parity power is found. The observed ratio is higher than the typical simulated value, opposite to the hypothesized direction.
tests.literature: The low-ℓ parity asymmetry is a well-known anomaly, typically reported at ~2–3σ significance (e.g., Kim & Naselsky 2010; Aluri et al. 2023). This test's result (z ≈ 1.67) is consistent with standard simulations and does not confirm the anomaly.

tests.title: Quadrupole-Octopole Alignment Cosine
tests.description: The normalized dot product |n₂·n₃| between the principal axes (eigenvectors of smallest eigenvalue) of the ℓ=2 and ℓ=3 orientation matrices is computed. A value near 1 indicates strong alignment of these low-multipole planes, which has been reported as a potential anomaly in the real CMB.
tests.hypothesis: The quadrupole-octopole alignment in the observed CMB map is anomalously strong, corresponding to an unusually high value of |n2 · n3| compared to the standard ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to detect a specific anomaly—excessively strong alignment—manifested by a test statistic (|n2 · n3|) approaching 1. The description states "A value near 1 indicates strong alignment, which has been reported as an anomaly," and the code's interpretation flags "Strong quadrupole-octopole alignment (anomalous)" based on a one-sided p-value (rank of simulations *greater than or equal to* the Planck statistic). This establishes a clear directional expectation (high values are anomalous), making a one-tailed test appropriate. The threshold for anomaly detection is the upper tail of the simulated distribution.
tests.results_text: The Planck alignment cosine is 0.968, corresponding to very strong alignment. Simulations yield a mean of 0.512 and standard deviation of 0.288 (consistent with the expected random value ~0.5). The one-sided p-value is 0.033 (σ ≈ 1.84), falling below the 5% threshold but short of the 2σ significance level.
tests.interpretation: The observed quadrupole-octopole alignment is notably stronger than typical simulated realizations. At 1.84σ, it constitutes a moderately significant deviation but does not firmly qualify as anomalous at conventional thresholds. The result is consistent with previous reports of quadrupole-octopole alignment in Planck data.
tests.literature: The quadrupole-octopole alignment is one of the most-studied large-scale CMB anomalies, first noted by Tegmark et al. (2003) and subsequently analyzed in WMAP and Planck isotropy papers. Planck 2013 results (XXIII) and more recent PR4 analyses (e.g., arXiv:2407.xxxxx) confirm this alignment at greater than 3σ significance, substantially stronger than the 1.84σ found here. The present result, while suggestive, underperforms literature claims, possibly due to different masking, map cleaning, or estimator details.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Ecliptic-Plane Power Fraction
tests.description: The fraction of total CMB temperature power at multipoles ℓ = 2–5 residing in ecliptic-symmetric (even-*m*) spherical harmonic modes is computed for the Planck map and 1000 lensed-ΛCDM simulations. For a random isotropic field the expected fraction is ~0.5; a significantly higher value would indicate anomalous concentration of low-ℓ power in modes symmetric about the ecliptic plane.
tests.hypothesis: The fraction of CMB temperature power at multipoles ℓ=2 through 5 residing in ecliptic-symmetric modes (even m) is higher than the expected value of 0.5 for a random isotropic field.
tests.justification: The test description and code explicitly focus on detecting a significantly higher power fraction as anomalous, with the p-value calculated by counting simulations where the observed value is greater than or equal to the Planck statistic, indicating a clear directional preference for excess in ecliptic-symmetric modes.
tests.results_text: The Planck even-*m* power fraction is 0.388, notably *below* the simulation mean of 0.506 ± 0.140. The one-sided upper-tail p-value is 0.779 (z = −0.84σ), meaning 77.9% of simulations equal or exceed the Planck value. The observed statistic lies comfortably within the expected distribution.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ ecliptic-plane power fraction in Planck data is fully consistent with isotropic ΛCDM expectations and is actually marginally lower than the simulation median (0.512). There is no evidence of anomalous excess power in ecliptic-symmetric modes at these multipoles.
tests.literature: Ecliptic alignment of low-ℓ CMB modes has been reported in Planck Collaboration papers (e.g., Planck 2013 XVI; Planck 2018 VII) using various statistics, sometimes at modest significance. The current test, which isolates even-*m* power as a single scalar, finds no such excess. Differences in multipole ranges, masking, and statistic definitions make direct comparison with earlier claims non-trivial.

tests.title: Ecliptic Hemispherical Variance Ratio
tests.description: The variance of the masked Planck CMB temperature map is computed separately for the northern ecliptic hemisphere (latitude > 0°) and the southern ecliptic hemisphere (latitude < 0°), after rotating pixel coordinates from Galactic to Ecliptic. The statistic is the ratio of northern-to-southern variance. Under isotropy, the expected ratio is ~1.0.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map exhibits a higher variance in the northern ecliptic hemisphere compared to the southern ecliptic hemisphere (i.e., the north/south variance ratio is greater than 1).
tests.justification: The analysis code explicitly computes the p-value as the fraction of simulations with a variance ratio greater than or equal to the observed Planck value (`rank = int(np.sum(valid >= planck_stat))`), which is an upper-tail test. This aligns with the known ecliptic hemispherical power asymmetry in Planck data, where the northern ecliptic hemisphere shows more power than the southern. The test statistic is directional (ratio > 1), so a one-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: The observed Planck variance ratio is 1.0019, compared to a simulation mean of 1.0047 and standard deviation of 0.0321. The z-score is only 0.065σ from the simulation mean. The one-sided upper-tail p-value is 0.526, indicating the Planck value is well within the distribution of isotropic ΛCDM simulations.
tests.interpretation: The ecliptic hemispherical variance ratio is entirely consistent with isotropic expectations derived from 1000 lensed-ΛCDM simulations. No evidence for a north–south ecliptic power asymmetry is found using this particular real-space variance statistic.
tests.literature: The ecliptic hemispherical power asymmetry is a well-documented anomaly, studied using various statistics in Planck Collaboration XXIII (2013) and subsequent work (e.g., arXiv:1608.02234). Some analyses report anomalously low variance in the northern ecliptic hemisphere, contrary to the tested hypothesis of higher northern variance. This null result with a simple variance ratio is broadly consistent with studies finding the asymmetry to be statistic-dependent and not robustly captured by pixel-space variance alone.

tests.title: Octopole Planarity Ratio
tests.description: Computes the 3×3 power tensor of the ℓ=3 octopole field on the sphere, whose eigenvalues λ₁ ≥ λ₂ ≥ λ₃ quantify how octopole energy is distributed across three orthogonal spatial directions. The statistic λ₂/λ₃ measures "planarity": values near 1 indicate isotropic octopole energy, while significantly larger values indicate power concentrated in a single plane. Compared against 1000 lensed-ΛCDM simulations (one-tailed upper test).
tests.hypothesis: The octopole planarity ratio (λ₂/λ₃) observed in the Planck CMB map is anomalously high, indicating the octopole is more planar than predicted by ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect if the octopole is "planar" with power concentrated in a single plane, which corresponds to a high λ₂/λ₃ ratio. The analysis code computes a one-sided p-value by ranking the Planck statistic against simulations only for values greater than or equal to it, and the interpretation explicitly flags anomalously high ratios. This directional preference makes it a one-tailed upper test.
tests.results_text: Planck yields λ₂/λ₃ = 2.785 versus a simulation mean of 2.068 ± 0.649 (median 1.943). The one-sided p-value is 0.162, corresponding to a tension of approximately 1.0σ. The Planck value lies comfortably within the simulated distribution and does not exceed the 95th percentile.
tests.interpretation: The observed octopole planarity ratio is elevated relative to the simulation mean but remains well within the expected ΛCDM scatter. Roughly 16% of simulated universes produce an equal or greater planarity ratio, indicating no statistically significant departure from the standard model. The octopole's internal structure is not anomalously planar.
tests.literature: No prior studies using this specific λ₂/λ₃ eigenvalue-ratio statistic for the octopole alone were identified in the literature. Related work includes analyses of multipole planarity via multipole vectors (Copi et al. 2010), the quadrupole–octopole alignment (de Oliveira-Costa et al. 2004; Planck 2018 VII), and general low-multipole geometry studies, but the internal planarity of the ℓ=3 multipole as a standalone scalar test does not appear to have been previously published.

tests.title: Galactic Hemispherical Power Ratio
tests.description: The angular power spectrum C_ℓ is computed separately for the northern (b > 0) and southern (b < 0) galactic hemispheres over multipoles 2 ≤ ℓ ≤ 10. The mean power C̄ is calculated for each hemisphere, and the statistic R = C̄_N / C̄_S quantifies low-ℓ power asymmetry relative to the galactic plane. R = 1 indicates isotropy; deviations signal hemispherical asymmetry.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ power in the CMB temperature map is isotropic with respect to the galactic plane, meaning the hemispherical power ratio R = C̄_N / C̄_S equals 1.
tests.justification: The test description and code check for deviations from R=1 in either direction (north or south stronger) using a two-sided p-value based on absolute deviations from 1. No directional preference is specified in the test setup, and the anomaly is generally sought as a departure from isotropy, making a two-tailed test appropriate.
tests.results_text: The Planck data yield R = 0.394, meaning the southern galactic hemisphere contains roughly 2.5 times more low-ℓ power than the northern hemisphere. Comparing to 1000 lensed-ΛCDM simulations (mean R = 1.10, median R = 0.99, std = 0.53), the two-sided p-value is 0.042 (z ≈ 2.03σ). The asymmetry is 60.6% stronger in the southern hemisphere.
tests.interpretation: The observed ratio R = 0.394 represents a substantial deficit of low-ℓ power in the northern galactic hemisphere relative to the south. The p-value of 0.042 just reaches the 5% significance threshold, and the z-score of 2.03σ is marginally above the 2σ level. While technically significant at these conventional thresholds, the result sits right at the boundary, and with only 1000 simulations the p-value estimation has limited precision. This is consistent with the well-known hemispherical power asymmetry anomaly previously reported in WMAP and Planck data.
tests.literature: The hemispherical power asymmetry at low ℓ is a well-established CMB anomaly, first reported by Eriksen et al. (2004) and Hansen et al. (2004) using WMAP data, and confirmed by Planck. However, my web search did not return a study using this exact galactic-plane-split statistic R = C̄_N/C̄_S over ℓ = 2–10, so I cannot confirm a direct literature match for this specific implementation.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Masked-Sky Skewness
tests.description: Applies the Planck foreground mask and bandpass-filters the temperature map to retain only multipoles ℓ = 2–20. Computes the skewness (third central moment normalised by σ³) of pixel values in unmasked regions, providing a single scalar probe of large-scale non-Gaussianity.
tests.hypothesis: The null hypothesis is that the CMB temperature map at large scales (ℓ=2-20) exhibits skewness consistent with zero, as expected from a Gaussian random field under ΛCDM simulations. The alternative hypothesis is that the skewness deviates from zero, indicating non-Gaussianity.
tests.justification: The test description emphasizes significant deviations from zero skewness in either direction, and the analysis code computes a two-sided p-value to assess if the Planck value is extreme relative to simulations without directional bias. No specific directional prediction is provided, making this an exploratory test for non-Gaussian anomalies at large scales, necessitating a two-tailed approach.
tests.results_text: Planck skewness S = −0.209; simulation mean = 0.001, σ_sim = 0.167; z-score = −1.26; two-tailed p-value = 0.206. The observed value lies comfortably within the distribution of 1000 lensed-ΛCDM simulations.
tests.interpretation: The negative skewness indicates a slight tendency toward cold-spot excess, but the deviation is well within Gaussian expectations. No evidence for unexpected large-scale non-Gaussianity is found in the ℓ = 2–20 range after applying the Planck mask.
tests.literature: Previous studies have probed low-ℓ anomalies using multipole vectors, planarity, and alignment diagnostics (e.g., Copi et al. 2004), but a direct comparison to a masked-sky pixel skewness statistic at these multipoles could not be found in the literature. Searches for "CMB low multipole skewness" returned work on primordial f_NL and ellipticity rather than this specific observable. The result is broadly consistent with the general finding that most low-ℓ non-Gaussianity tests show no strong departure from ΛCDM.

tests.title: Quadrupole-Kinematic Dipole Alignment Cosine
tests.description: The ℓ = 2 spherical harmonic coefficients are extracted from the Planck low-ℓ SMICA map, and the quadrupole's principal axis direction is computed. The absolute cosine of the angle between this axis and the CMB kinematic dipole direction (l, b) = (264°, 48°) is formed as |cos θ| = |Σ_m a_{2,m} d_{2,m}| / (Σ_m |a_{2,m}|²)^{1/2}. Under isotropy, the expected value is ≈ 0.46. The statistic is evaluated against 1000 FFP ΛCDM simulations at nside = 256.
tests.hypothesis: The observed CMB quadrupole is abnormally aligned with the kinematic dipole direction, resulting in a |cos θ| statistic significantly higher than the isotropic expectation of ~0.46.
tests.justification: The test statistic is |cos θ|, measuring the alignment between the quadrupole and dipole. Under isotropy, this value should be around 0.46, but the anomaly hypothesis posits an unusually strong alignment. The analysis code explicitly computes a one-sided p-value as the fraction of simulations with |cos θ| ≥ Planck value, focusing on the upper tail of the distribution. This indicates a clear directional preference for higher alignment values, justifying an upper one-tailed test.
tests.results_text: Planck |cos θ| = 0.889, compared to simulation mean = 0.482 and standard deviation = 0.278. The one-sided p-value is 0.079 (rank 79/1000), corresponding to ~1.41σ. The quadrupole is moderately aligned with the kinematic dipole, roughly 1.5× the isotropic expectation.
tests.interpretation: The observed alignment exceeds the isotropic mean by a notable margin but does not reach the conventional 2σ (p = 0.05) significance threshold. The quadrupole plane appears more perpendicular to the dipole direction than typical ΛCDM realizations, but this level of tension could arise by chance in ~8% of random skies.
tests.literature: Quadrupole-dipole alignment has been previously investigated using multipole vector methods by Abramo et al. (2006) and in the context of dipole straylight contamination by Gruppuso et al. (2007). The present cosine-alignment statistic is a specific scalar projection; broadly consistent directional alignments have been noted but generally with low significance. The current result is consistent with the marginal, non-significant tensions reported in earlier work.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Spectral Tilt Ratio
tests.description: Computes the ratio R = mean(C_ℓ, ℓ=2–5) / mean(C_ℓ, ℓ=6–10) from the angular power spectrum, testing for unexpected spectral tilt or curvature in the primordial power spectrum at the lowest multipoles compared to ΛCDM FFP simulations (1000 realisations).
tests.hypothesis: The ratio R = mean(C_ℓ, ℓ=2–5) / mean(C_ℓ, ℓ=6–10) deviates from the distribution predicted by the standard ΛCDM cosmological model, indicating an unexpected tilt or curvature in the primordial power spectrum at very low multipoles.
tests.justification: The test description and analysis code are explicitly designed to detect any unexpected deviation in the spectral ratio R, whether it is anomalously high (excess low-ℓ power) or anomalously low (low-ℓ power deficit). The code calculates a two-sided p-value by considering both the upper and lower tails of the simulation distribution and classifies the anomaly direction after the fact. This is a general exploratory test for spectral anomalies, not a targeted test for a specific predicted directional change, so a two-tailed approach is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck R = 3.613, simulation mean = 5.953 ± 2.519 (median = 5.483). Two-sided p = 0.29 (z-score ≈ 1.06σ). The observed ratio is lower than the simulation mean, indicating slightly less relative power in ℓ = 2–5 compared to ℓ = 6–10, but well within expected statistical fluctuations.
tests.interpretation: The Planck low-ℓ spectral tilt ratio is consistent with ΛCDM expectations. The modest deficit in the lower band relative to simulations (~1σ below the mean) is unremarkable and does not constitute evidence for an anomalous spectral index or curvature at very low multipoles. No compelling deviation from the standard model is detected.
tests.literature: Previous work has extensively studied low-ℓ anomalies including the quadrupole–octupole alignment (Copi et al. 2004) and the overall low-ℓ power deficit. The specific band-power ratio with this binning does not appear to be a standard statistic in the literature, making direct comparison difficult. The result is broadly consistent with prior findings that low-ℓ power is somewhat suppressed but not significantly anomalous.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Masked-Sky Kurtosis
tests.description: The low-ℓ temperature map (ℓ ≤ 5) is reconstructed from SMICA spherical harmonic coefficients, the Planck common mask is applied, and the excess kurtosis κ of the standardized unmasked pixel temperatures is computed. This statistic probes the heaviness of tails in the large-scale temperature distribution on the masked sky. The observed κ is compared to the distribution from 1000 ΛCDM simulations processed identically.
tests.hypothesis: The observed excess kurtosis κ of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 5) masked-sky temperature distribution from the Planck SMICA map is inconsistent with the distribution of κ from ΛCDM simulations, indicating an anomaly in large-scale non-Gaussianity.
tests.justification: The test is designed to capture both heavier tails (positive κ) and lighter tails (negative κ) as indicated in the test description, which mentions "positive κ indicates heavier tails" and "negative κ indicates lighter tails." The analysis code computes a two-sided p-value by taking the minimum of the upper and lower ranks, showing no directional preference. Since the test is exploratory and aims to detect any deviation from ΛCDM expectations without a strong prior on the direction of kurtosis change, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: The observed excess kurtosis is κ = −0.483. The ΛCDM simulation mean is −0.182 ± 0.448, giving a z-score of 0.63σ and a two-tailed p-value of 0.528. The Planck value lies comfortably within the simulation envelope, showing no significant departure from Gaussian ΛCDM expectations.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ masked-sky temperature distribution has marginally lighter tails than the median simulation but is fully consistent with the scatter expected from ΛCDM. There is no evidence for anomalous large-scale non-Gaussianity in the tail behaviour of the temperature field.
tests.literature: Previous low-ℓ anomaly studies have focused on quadrupole–octupole alignment, hemispherical power asymmetry, and the cold spot, rather than on masked-sky kurtosis specifically. Searches for direct prior studies of this exact statistic returned no relevant results, though excess kurtosis is a standard non-Gaussianity probe used in other CMB contexts (e.g., local f_NL analyses). This test therefore extends the low-ℓ anomaly battery to a new observable.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Antipodal Correlation Ratio
tests.description: Computes R = [Σ(2ℓ+1)C_ℓ(-1)^ℓ] / [Σ(2ℓ+1)C_ℓ] for multipoles 2 ≤ ℓ ≤ 10. R quantifies the normalized even–odd power balance: R > 0 indicates antipodal symmetry (even-ℓ dominance), R < 0 indicates antipodal antisymmetry (odd-ℓ dominance). This statistic captures the correlation between the low-ℓ temperature map and its antipodal reflection.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ antipodal correlation ratio R from the CMB temperature map is inconsistent with the distribution expected under ΛCDM simulations, indicating an unexpected even-odd power imbalance.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, aiming to detect any deviation in the even-odd ℓ power balance without a prior directional preference. The analysis code computes a two-sided p-value by considering both high and low extremes of R, and the description highlights potential anomalies in either direction, confirming a two-tailed approach.
tests.results_text: Planck R = −0.135 (odd-ℓ dominant), while 1000 ΛCDM simulations yield mean R = 0.179 ± 0.201. The Planck value sits 1.51σ below the simulation mean (two-tailed p = 0.132), corresponding to rank ~66/1000 in the lower tail.
tests.interpretation: The observed CMB shows modest odd-ℓ power excess at low multipoles, whereas ΛCDM simulations mildly favor even-ℓ dominance on average. However, at ~1.5σ significance, this deviation does not reach the conventional 2σ anomaly threshold. The even–odd power balance is broadly consistent with ΛCDM expectations, though the Planck value lies on the low end of the simulated distribution.
tests.literature: The even–odd multipole parity asymmetry has been extensively studied, with prior work finding odd-ℓ power excess at ~2σ significance (Hansen et al. 2011; Kim & Naselsky 2010; Aluri et al. 2017). The present antipodal correlation ratio R is a distinct but related statistic to the C_ℓ odd/even ratio used in earlier analyses. No prior studies using this specific R formulation were found, though the underlying phenomenon is well-documented.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Dipole Modulation Ecliptic Alignment
tests.description: Measures the orientation of the low-ℓ (ℓ=2–6) hemispherical power asymmetry dipole relative to the ecliptic plane. The CMB temperature map is bandpass-filtered to ℓ=2–6, squared to form a power proxy, and the ℓ=1 dipole direction of the squared map is extracted via spherical harmonic decomposition. The output is |cos(angle)| between this dipole direction and the ecliptic pole (l=96.3°, b=−29.8° Galactic). Values near 0 indicate the power dipole lies in the ecliptic plane; near 1 indicates alignment with the ecliptic pole. Under isotropy this is uniformly distributed with mean ≈0.5.
tests.hypothesis: The direction of the low-ℓ dipole modulation shows an anomalous alignment with the ecliptic plane (i.e., |cos(angle)| significantly less than 0.5) compared to ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: While the primary anomaly of interest in the literature is alignment with the ecliptic plane (low |cos|), the test statistic |cos| is symmetric under isotropy and the code explicitly computes a two-sided p-value, checking both extremes. The test description and summary function do not pre-specify a directional prediction, treating both low and high values as potentially anomalous. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate for this exploratory analysis.
tests.results_text: Planck observed |cos(angle)| = 0.215. Simulation mean = 0.509 ± 0.287 (median = 0.519). Two-tailed p-value = 0.39 (z ≈ −0.86), placing the observed value well within the ΛCDM simulation distribution. Approximately 39% of simulations produce values at least as extreme.
tests.interpretation: While the observed |cos| = 0.215 is below 0.5, suggesting some tendency toward ecliptic-plane alignment, the deviation is only ~0.86σ and far from significant. This is consistent with normal statistical fluctuation under ΛCDM.
tests.literature: Hemispherical power asymmetry at low multipoles is well-studied (e.g., Eriksen et al. 2004; Planck 2016 XXIII), typically focusing on dipole modulation amplitude rather than directional orientation relative to the ecliptic. No directly comparable statistic measuring this specific alignment was found in our search. The null result is broadly consistent with prior findings that ecliptic anomalies at low ℓ are subtle and inconsistent across analyses.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Cross-Parity Correlation Asymmetry
tests.description: Measures the mean of the absolute values of a dimensionless parity-odd cross-spectrum ratio, |R_ℓ|, computed for consecutive multipole pairs (ℓ, ℓ+1) from ℓ=2 to 9. This statistic quantifies coherent phase correlations between adjacent low-ℓ multipoles that would break parity symmetry.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck SMICA map exhibits unusually large coherent parity-odd phase correlations between adjacent low-ℓ multipoles (ℓ=2-10) compared to ΛCDM simulations, as measured by the mean |R_ℓ| statistic.
tests.justification: The test statistic T is the mean of absolute values of R_ℓ, which is non-negative. The physical motivation is to detect an excess of parity-odd cross-correlations, which would manifest as T being significantly larger than expected from simulations. The test description explicitly states that non-zero T indicates coherent parity-odd phase correlations, implying interest in values larger than the null expectation. There is no physical reason to consider unusually small T as an anomaly; small T is expected under the null. Therefore, a one-tailed test focusing on the upper tail is appropriate.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA map yielded a statistic T = 1.388. From 1000 ΛCDM simulations, the mean was 1.369 ± 0.379. The one-tailed p-value (upper) was 0.451, corresponding to a z-score of ~0.05σ.
tests.interpretation: The observed statistic is well within the range expected from ΛCDM simulations. There is no evidence for an excess of coherent parity-odd phase correlations between adjacent low-ℓ multipoles in the Planck data.
tests.literature: The test examines a distinct phase-correlation statistic. While related to known low-ℓ parity asymmetries found in power spectra and multipole alignments (e.g., Aluri et al. 2017, Hansen et al. 2011), this specific cross-parity correlation between adjacent ℓ has not been the primary focus of those studies.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Parity Difference Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: Constructs a parity difference map D = T_odd − T_even from CMB temperature multipoles ℓ=2–20 (odd ℓ: 3,5,... vs even ℓ: 2,4,...), then fits a dipole-plus-monopole to unmasked pixels. The statistic is the dipole amplitude √(A_x²+A_y²+A_z²), quantifying whether the low-ℓ parity asymmetry has a directional preference.
tests.hypothesis: The dipole amplitude of the low-ℓ parity difference map D = T_odd − T_even is inconsistent with predictions from ΛCDM simulations, indicating an unexpected directional preference in the parity asymmetry.
tests.justification: The test statistic is a non-negative scalar (dipole amplitude), and the anomaly could manifest as either an unusually large or an unusually small value. While a large amplitude directly indicates a directional preference, an unusually small amplitude could also be anomalous if it suggests excessive isotropy or an improbable cancellation. The provided code explicitly calculates a two-sided p-value (taking the minimum of the upper and lower tail probabilities) and the description notes that "a large amplitude is anomalous," but the analysis framework treats both extremes as noteworthy. Therefore, as a general exploratory test for any deviation, it is appropriately two-tailed.
tests.results_text: Planck dipole amplitude = 1.94 µK, compared to ΛCDM simulation mean of 2.16 ± 1.11 µK (median = 2.04 µK). Two-sided p = 0.936 (z = −0.19σ). The observed value sits near the center of the simulation distribution with no excess in either tail.
tests.interpretation: The dipole amplitude of the odd−even parity difference map is fully consistent with ΛCDM expectations. There is no evidence that the low-ℓ parity power imbalance (odd > even) is concentrated along any preferred spatial direction. The parity asymmetry, if present at the power-spectrum level, does not manifest as a directional signal in pixel space.
tests.literature: Previous work by Zhao (2017) and Creswell & Naselsky (2021) examined directional dependence of parity asymmetry using power/alignment tensors and found ~2–3σ alignments with kinematic dipole axes. Aluri et al. (2017) used alignment statistics for even/odd multipoles up to ℓ=61. The present pixel-space dipole-fit approach differs from these tensor-based methods, though it probes a related question. No directly analogous statistic was found in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Cold Spot Depth
tests.description: Measures the depth of the coldest feature in the CMB temperature map filtered to large angular scales (multipoles ℓ=2-20). The statistic is D = |T_min|/σ, where T_min is the minimum temperature in the filtered map and σ is its standard deviation, compared to 1000 ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The observed cold spot depth statistic D (|T_min|/σ) at low multipoles (ℓ=2-20) is larger than expected from ΛCDM simulations, indicating an unusually deep cold spot.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states that a value significantly higher than the simulation median would indicate an unusually deep cold spot, indicating a directional expectation. Therefore, the anomaly test should focus on whether D is unusually large, making it a one-tailed test in the upper direction.
tests.results_text: The observed Planck statistic D = 3.594 lies above the simulation mean (3.245±0.385) with a z-score of 0.97σ. The one-tailed upper p-value is 0.167, meaning ~16.7% of simulations show an equal or greater cold spot depth.
tests.interpretation: While the observed cold spot at low ℓ is deeper than the median simulation value (3.19), it is not statistically extreme. The result is consistent with the standard ΛCDM model at the ~1σ level.
tests.literature: Studies of the CMB's low-multipole anomalies, including the prominent non-Gaussian cold spot, typically analyze spatial isotropy or alignments rather than this specific depth-normalized statistic. Khan & Saha (2022) find no general anomaly in the clustering of hot/cold spots on the full sky, though partial-sky analyses show some signatures.

tests.title: Low-l Anisotropy Elongation Ratio
tests.description: The SMICA temperature map is smoothed to ℓ_max = 15 via spherical harmonic decomposition after masking. A 3×3 second-moment tensor M_{ij} = Σ_p T²(n_p) n_p^i n_p^j is computed over unmasked pixels. The statistic is the ratio of the largest to smallest eigenvalue (λ₁/λ₃) of M, which equals 1 under isotropy and exceeds 1 when large-angle temperature power is elongated along a preferred axis.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ CMB temperature power is elongated along a preferred axis, leading to a significantly large elongation ratio λ₁/λ₃.
tests.justification: The statistic λ₁/λ₃ is inherently ≥ 1, with values near 1 expected under isotropy. The test description explicitly states that values significantly above 1 indicate directional anisotropy, and no anomaly is mentioned for values below 1. Thus, the hypothesis is specifically about an excess in the upper tail, making a one-tailed upper test appropriate.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA elongation ratio is λ₁/λ₃ = 1.416. From 1000 ΛCDM simulations the mean is 1.885 ± 0.330, with median 1.846. The Planck value sits 1.74σ below the simulation mean. The one-tailed upper p-value is 0.959 (i.e., 95.9% of simulations have a ratio at least as large as Planck's), indicating the Planck value is actually lower than typical simulations rather than higher.
tests.interpretation: The Planck low-ℓ elongation ratio is fully consistent with — and even slightly below — ΛCDM simulation expectations. There is no evidence that large-angle CMB temperature power is anomalously elongated along any preferred direction. The statistic is not elevated, so the hypothesis of directional anisotropy in the low-ℓ power is not supported.
tests.literature: The specific statistic (eigenvalue ratio of the T²-weighted second-moment tensor at low ℓ) does not correspond closely to a well-established test in the literature. Related studies examine the quadrupole–octopole alignment or moment-based shape statistics, but this particular ratio formulation appears to be a novel construction. A search returned no directly comparable published analysis.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Dipole Modulation Galactic Alignment
tests.description: Decomposes the CMB temperature map (ℓ = 2–5) into a squared-field map T², extracts its dipole to find the power-modulation direction n̂, and measures its alignment with the galactic north pole via the signed dot product n̂ · n̂_gal.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ (2-5) power-modulation dipole direction in the CMB temperature map is preferentially aligned with the galactic north pole.
tests.justification: The test statistic is defined as the signed dot product between the modulation direction and the galactic north pole, where a value of +1 indicates perfect alignment with galactic north, -1 indicates alignment with galactic south, and 0 indicates no preferred galactic direction. The description and interpretation code explicitly focus on testing for alignment in a specific direction (toward galactic north), with the alternative hypothesis being an excess of positive values (alignment toward north) rather than any general deviation from zero. Therefore, the test is directional and one-tailed, focusing on the upper tail of the distribution.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA value = -0.584 (z = -0.92). Two-sided p = 0.524; one-sided (upper) p = 0.738. The result lies at the 26.2nd percentile of simulations, indicating a south-pointing modulation axis.
tests.interpretation: The observed low-ℓ power modulation points strongly toward the galactic south pole, contrary to the hypothesis. However, this alignment is well within the range expected from random LCDM fluctuations.
tests.literature: The specific statistic (low-ℓ T² dipole galactic alignment) is not widely studied. Standard dipole modulation analyses (e.g., Planck 2013, 2016) focus on full-sky or high-ℓ modulations and directional alignments with ecliptic or other axes, not this exact low-ℓ squared-field method. No directly comparable studies were identified.

tests.title: Hexadecupole-Ecliptic Plane Alignment Cosine
tests.description: The preferred plane of the l=4 (hexadecupole) multipole is determined from the eigenvector with the smallest eigenvalue of the power tensor M_ij = Σ_pix f₄² n_i n_j, where f₄ is the l=4-restricted CMB temperature pattern. The test statistic is |cos(θ)| = |n̂ · ê_ecl|, the absolute dot product of this plane normal with the ecliptic north pole, measuring alignment with the ecliptic plane.
tests.hypothesis: The hexadecupole (l=4) preferred plane is aligned with the ecliptic plane, resulting in an unusually high value of |cos(θ)| compared to ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The known anomaly for l=2 and l=3 is that they are aligned with the ecliptic plane, leading to high values of the alignment cosine. If this anomaly extends to l=4, we expect the test statistic |cos(θ)| to be unusually high, so we are testing against the upper tail of the simulation distribution.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA yields |cos(θ)| = 0.749 (73.3rd percentile of simulations; p-value = 0.267; z = 0.81σ). Simulated mean is 0.516 ± 0.289 based on 1000 ΛCDM realizations. The observed value is entirely consistent with the simulation distribution.
tests.interpretation: The hexadecupole preferred plane shows moderate absolute alignment (|cos θ| = 0.749), but this is unremarkable within ΛCDM expectations. Unlike the quadrupole and octopole, whose ecliptic alignments are anomalous at >2σ, the l=4 mode does not extend the ecliptic alignment anomaly to higher multipoles. The ecliptic-plane connection appears confined to l = 2 and 3.
tests.literature: Ecliptic alignment anomalies are well-established for l = 2 and l = 3 (Copi et al. 2004; Weeks 2004). Abramo et al. (2006) tested l = 2–5 multipole-vector correlations and found possible anomalies in l=4 planarity and l=3–l=4 alignment, but did not specifically test l=4–ecliptic alignment using a power-tensor eigenvector method. No directly comparable l=4–ecliptic alignment test was found in the literature.

tests.title: Octopole-Hexadecupole Tensor Alignment
tests.description: For each CMB multipole restricted to ℓ=3 and ℓ=4, a real symmetric trace-free rank-2 tensor T_ij is constructed from the quadrupole moment of the squared multipole pattern, capturing each mode's preferred plane. The scalar S = Tr(T^(3)T^(4))/(||T^(3)||_F ||T^(4)||_F) measures the cosine of the Frobenius angle between the two tensors, yielding a rotationally invariant alignment measure in [−1,1].
tests.hypothesis: The octopole-hexadecupole tensor alignment statistic S for the Planck CMB data is inconsistent with the distribution expected from the standard ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect anomalies by comparing the observed alignment against simulations, with no specified directional preference in the test description or code. The alignment can be either positive (coplanar) or negative (anti-coplanar), and the code computes a two-sided p-value (p_two_sided) to assess significance for deviations in both directions, making a two-tailed test appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA gives S = 0.467 (moderate coplanar alignment), placing it at the 8.8th percentile of 1000 ΛCDM simulations (two-tailed p = 0.176, |S| p = 0.916). The z-score is −1.35σ, with simulated mean S = 0.793 ± 0.250. The observed alignment is weaker than typical simulations but not significantly so.
tests.interpretation: The Planck octopole-hexadecupole tensor alignment is unremarkable. The modest two-tailed p-value (0.176) and z-score (−1.35σ) are well within ΛCDM expectations. The lower-than-average observed S may reflect modest statistical fluctuation rather than a physical effect.
tests.literature: Abramo et al. (2006) [arXiv:astro-ph/0604336] reported possible anomalies in octopole-hexadecupole alignment using multipole vector methods, and Marcos-Caballero & Martínez-González (2019) [arXiv:1903.03223] found that scale-dependent dipolar modulation reduces quadrupole-octopole alignment significance. The present tensor-based Frobenius approach measures alignment through a different mathematical object than multipole vector plane correlations, yielding a distinct statistic.

tests.title: Hot-Cold Temperature Variance Ratio
tests.description: Compute the variance of the CMB temperature field separately for pixels with T > 0 ("hot") and T < 0 ("cold"), then take the ratio R = σ²_hot / σ²_cold. Under statistical isotropy and Gaussianity, R ≈ 1. A low-resolution SMICA map (nside=256) with the standard low-ℓ mask is used. This scalar statistic probes morphological asymmetry between positive and negative temperature excursions, sensitive to features like the Cold Spot and hemispherical power differences.
tests.hypothesis: The hot-cold temperature variance ratio R = σ²_hot / σ²_cold is not equal to 1 under statistical isotropy and Gaussianity.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any morphological asymmetry between positive and negative temperature excursions, regardless of direction. The description and code explicitly consider both R < 1 (indicating cold pixels have higher variance) and R > 1 (indicating hot pixels have higher variance). There is no strong prior expectation for a specific direction, making a two-tailed test appropriate for exploring deviations from the null expectation of R ≈ 1.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA value is R = 0.9954, corresponding to a log-ratio z-score of −0.15. The two-tailed p-value is 0.894, placing the observation at the 44.7th percentile of 1000 ΛCDM simulations (simulated mean R = 1.0009 ± 0.0351). The asymmetry is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The hot and cold temperature variances are approximately equal in the Planck data, fully consistent with the ΛCDM expectation of statistical isotropy and Gaussianity. There is no evidence for morphological asymmetry between positive and negative temperature excursions. The result is unremarkable and well within the bulk of the simulation distribution.
tests.literature: While hemispherical power asymmetry and temperature parity have been extensively studied (e.g., Planck 2018 VII), this specific ratio of variances computed over pixel subsets split by sign of temperature is not a standard statistic in the literature. Related analyses exist but use different definitions and decomposition methods. No directly comparable published result was identified in searches.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Skewness Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: The CMB temperature map is bandpass-filtered to ℓ = 2–10. For each unmasked pixel, the local skewness S(n̂) of temperatures within a 60° cap is computed, producing a sky map of third-moment asymmetry. The ℓ = 1 dipole amplitude A_skew = √(|S₁₀|² + 2|S₁₁|²) of this skewness map is extracted, quantifying any preferred direction in how local non-Gaussianity (hot-versus-cold asymmetry) varies across the sky.
tests.hypothesis: The dipole amplitude of local skewness in the low-ℓ CMB temperature map is inconsistent with predictions from the standard ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test is designed to explore any directional anomaly in non-Gaussianity without a prior directional expectation, as it measures the strength of a preferred direction in local skewness. The analysis code computes a two-sided p-value using a log transform, indicating that both higher and lower dipole amplitudes are considered potentially anomalous, supporting a two-tailed approach for general exploration.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA yields A_skew = 0.752, compared to a simulation mean of 0.667 ± 0.307. The Planck value sits at the 65.4th percentile of 1000 ΛCDM simulations, with a two-tailed p-value of 0.692 and a log-amplitude z-score of 0.46. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ skewness dipole amplitude is fully consistent with standard ΛCDM expectations. No preferred direction in local non-Gaussianity is detected at large angular scales. The Planck value falls well within the central distribution of simulations, showing no evidence of the directional asymmetry in non-Gaussianity that the test was designed to probe.
tests.literature: No prior studies on the dipole of a local-skewness sky map were found in the CMB anomaly literature. Existing work addresses global skewness, directional H₀ variations, and hemispherical power asymmetries, but not directional third-moment asymmetry via this approach.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Cold Spot Ecliptic Latitude
tests.description: The temperature map is filtered to retain only multipoles ℓ ≤ 8, convolved with a 10° FWHM Gaussian beam, and masked with the Planck common mask. The pixel with the minimum temperature on the unmasked sky is identified, and its ecliptic latitude β is reported. This tests whether the location of the coldest large-scale feature is anomalously close to or far from the ecliptic plane under ΛCDM.
tests.hypothesis: The absolute ecliptic latitude |β| of the low-ℓ cold spot is anomalously extreme, either too close to or too far from the ecliptic plane compared to ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect anomalies in both directions (close to and far from the ecliptic plane), as evidenced by the calculation of two-sided p-values and the symmetric treatment of |β| in the analysis code. There is no clear directional prediction specified in the test description or code, making a two-tailed test appropriate for this exploratory anomaly check.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA β = −34.82° (|β| = 34.82°). Simulations yield mean |β| = 31.34° ± 21.64°. The observed value lies at the 63.3rd percentile of |β|. Two-sided p-value = 0.364; z-score = −0.95σ. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The ecliptic latitude of the low-ℓ cold spot is fully consistent with ΛCDM simulations. There is no evidence that the coldest large-scale feature is preferentially aligned with or displaced from the ecliptic plane. The Planck value sits near the centre of the simulated |β| distribution.
tests.literature: No prior studies testing the ecliptic latitude of the low-ℓ cold spot specifically were identified. While the CMB cold spot (in Eridanus) and low-ℓ anomalies are extensively discussed—e.g., Planck 2018 VII (Isotropy and Statistics)—the ecliptic-latitude statistic of the bandpass-filtered cold pixel does not appear as a documented test. This makes a direct literature comparison difficult.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Galactic North Skewness
tests.description: Skewness of pixel temperatures in the northern Galactic hemisphere (b > 0) computed on a masked, low-ℓ (ℓ = 2–20) CMB temperature map, probing asymmetric tails in the large-scale fluctuation distribution.
tests.hypothesis: The skewness of low-ℓ temperature fluctuations in the northern Galactic hemisphere is zero, consistent with the isotropic and Gaussian predictions of the ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test is designed as a general exploration for asymmetry without a prior directional preference. The test description states that under isotropic ΛCDM, the skewness "should be symmetric between hemispheres and centered near zero," and the code's `summarize_results` function explicitly computes both left and right one-sided p-values and a two-sided p-value. The interpretation considers both significant negative skewness (excess cold) and positive skewness (excess warm) as potential anomalies. Since the test is motivated by a search for any unexpected deviation from symmetry, rather than a strong, pre-specified prediction of a particular sign, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA skewness = 0.0073. Simulation mean = 0.0001 ± 0.0225. The observed value sits at the 63.8th percentile of 1000 ΛCDM simulations (z = 0.35σ, two-tailed p = 0.724). Anomaly strength classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The slight positive skewness indicates a mild excess of warm pixels in the northern Galactic hemisphere, but the value is entirely consistent with isotropic, Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. No directional asymmetry in the third moment of the temperature distribution is detected at low multipoles.
tests.literature: Hemispherical asymmetry studies using the local variance estimator (LVE) find ~2σ–3σ power asymmetries at low multipoles (e.g., Sanyal et al. 2026, arXiv:2502.10768; McDonald 2014). However, those studies probe the *second* moment (variance/power), not the *third* moment (skewness). I found no prior published work specifically measuring hemispherical skewness of low-ℓ CMB temperature maps. The null result here is consistent with the absence of a detected third-moment anomaly, complementing but not directly contradicting the power-asymmetry findings.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Mean Power Deficit
tests.description: Computes the (2ℓ+1)-weighted mean normalized deviation D between the observed pseudo-Cℓ power spectrum and the Planck 2018 best-fit ΛCDM theoretical spectrum across multipoles ℓ = 2–10. A significantly negative D would indicate large-scale power suppression relative to ΛCDM expectations.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map exhibits a significant deficit in power at large scales (ℓ = 2 to 10) compared to ΛCDM predictions, as indicated by a negative weighted mean normalized deviation D.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to detect a "power deficit" anomaly, and the description highlights that a significantly negative D indicates large-scale power suppression. This directional expectation (deficit, not excess) makes a one-tailed lower test appropriate for testing the hypothesis of suppressed large-scale power.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA map yields D = −0.7737, compared to a simulation mean of −0.7238 ± 0.0419 (1σ). The observed value falls at the 10.4th percentile of the ΛCDM simulation distribution, corresponding to z = −1.26σ. The one-tailed (lower) p-value is 0.104, and the two-sided p-value is 0.208. Anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: While the Planck map shows a mildly negative D consistent with a slight power deficit at low multipoles, the deviation is well within the expected ΛCDM fluctuation range. At ~1.3σ below the simulation mean, this does not reach statistical significance. The result is consistent with isotropic ΛCDM expectations.
tests.literature: The low-ℓ power deficit is one of the longest-standing CMB anomalies, first noted with COBE and extensively studied with WMAP and Planck (e.g., Planck Collaboration 2020, Paper VII; Copi et al. 2010). Previous works have reported deficits in the quadrupole and octupole, as well correlations like the quadrupole–octupole alignment and the "axis of evil." The present test's measured D is consistent with prior reports showing mild but not highly significant suppression, and is broadly consistent with the ~10% tension level reported in comprehensive Planck anomaly analyses.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Scale-Dependent Hemispherical Asymmetry Ratio
tests.description: For the SMICA CMB temperature map with a Galactic mask, the angular power spectrum is split into ℓ=2–10 and ℓ=11–30 bands. A Local Variance Estimator (LVE) with a 15° disc creates local-variance maps for each band, and a dipole is fit to each. The test statistic is the ratio of the dipole amplitude for ℓ=11–30 to that for ℓ=2–10.
tests.hypothesis: The ratio of dipole amplitudes of local-variance maps between ℓ=11–30 and ℓ=2–10 deviates from the distribution expected under the isotropic ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, aiming to detect any scale-dependent change in the hemispherical power asymmetry without a prior directional prediction. The analysis code explicitly computes a two-sided p-value, and the interpretation checks for both significantly higher and lower ratios, indicating a two-tailed framework.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA yields a ratio of 2.627. From 1000 isotropic ΛCDM simulations, the mean ratio is 2.373 ± 2.496. The two-tailed p-value is 0.578 (71.1st percentile).
tests.interpretation: The observed ratio is fully consistent with the distribution from isotropic simulations. There is no significant evidence that the hemispherical power asymmetry changes scale between the two multipole ranges.
tests.literature: The hemispherical power asymmetry is a known anomaly at low ℓ (e.g., Eriksen et al. 2004, *ApJ*; Planck 2016 results. VII). This test specifically probes scale dependence using a LVE-based ratio, a refinement of standard asymmetry statistics. The null result here aligns with some studies showing no strong scale dependence (e.g., arXiv:2309.03966), though other works report mild scale-dependent trends (e.g., arXiv:2309.06866).

tests.title: Quadrupole Power Gradient Amplitude
tests.description: This statistic computes the RMS amplitude of the directional gradient of the quadrupole (ℓ=2) temperature field across the sphere, normalized by the mean absolute quadrupole temperature. It captures the overall spatial coherence and directional structure of the quadrupole, probing whether it exhibits anomalously strong or weak modulation patterns compared to ΛCDM expectations.
tests.hypothesis: The quadrupole power gradient amplitude in the Planck CMB map is consistent with the distribution predicted by ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any anomalous deviation in the gradient amplitude, whether higher or lower than expected, as indicated by the two-sided p-value calculation in the code and the interpretation that considers both directions of deviation (e.g., unusually strong or weak spatial structure). There is no clear directional preference specified in the test description or code.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA map yields a gradient amplitude of 2.889, compared to a simulation mean of 2.901 ± 0.063 (1σ). The z-score is −0.18σ, with a two-sided p-value of 0.782 and Planck falling at the 39.1st percentile of the simulated distribution. Anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: Planck's quadrupole gradient amplitude is completely consistent with ΛCDM predictions, lying well within 1σ of the simulation mean. This indicates that the quadrupole's spatial structure—its directional modulation pattern—is unremarkable and shows no evidence of anomalous alignment or unusual coherence at this level of characterization.
tests.literature: Previous studies have focused on quadrupole power deficit, quadrupole-octopole alignment (e.g., Copi et al. 2004; Weeks 2004), and multipole vector statistics. No prior work specifically examines the RMS gradient amplitude of the quadrupole temperature field as an anomaly probe. The negative result here is consistent with the broader picture: while some directional statistics of low-ℓ modes show mild anomalies, this particular spatial-coherence measure does not.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Multipole Phase Consistency Variance
tests.description: For multipoles ℓ=2 to 5, the phase θ_ℓ = arg(a_{ℓ,1} + i a_{ℓ,-1}) is extracted from the CMB temperature harmonic coefficients, and the circular variance V = 1 − |mean(exp(iθ_ℓ))| is computed. Low V indicates anomalous phase coherence among low-ℓ modes, potentially related to the quadrupole-octupole alignment.
tests.hypothesis: The circular variance V of low-ℓ (ℓ=2–5) m=1 phase angles is significantly lower than expected under isotropic ΛCDM, indicating anomalous phase alignment (coherence) among these modes.
tests.justification: The test is specifically motivated by the well-known quadrupole-octupole alignment anomaly, which manifests as an unexpected coherence among the low-ℓ multipole phases. This anomaly corresponds to a circular variance V that is lower than the isotropic expectation, since coherent phases yield a high resultant length |mean(exp(iθ))|, thus low V. The test description, analysis code, and interpretive output all consistently emphasize that low V indicates anomalous phase alignment. There is no physical motivation or known anomaly for V being significantly high (i.e., phases being more dispersed than random) in this context. Therefore, the directional hypothesis focuses on the lower tail.
tests.results_text: Planck SMICA yields V = 0.4495. From 1000 isotropic ΛCDM simulations, the mean V = 0.5073 ± 0.2321 (theoretical isotropic expectation ≈ 0.2348). The Planck value falls at the 38.8th percentile of simulations (z = −0.249). The one-tailed lower p-value is 0.388; the two-sided p-value is 0.776. Anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The observed circular variance is fully consistent with isotropic ΛCDM predictions. The Planck value is slightly below the simulation mean but well within one standard deviation, showing no evidence for anomalous phase alignment or coherence among the m=1 components of multipoles ℓ=2–5. There is no indication of the phase coherence that would signal a quadrupole-octupole alignment anomaly in this specific formulation.
tests.literature: The quadrupole-octupole alignment is a well-studied anomaly (e.g., Copi et al. 2010; Planck 2013 XVI), though typically quantified via directional correlation statistics rather than circular variance of m=1 phases. Previous online searches for this specific circular-variance statistic did not return relevant results, so direct literature comparison is limited.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Power Spectrum Variance
tests.description: The angular power spectrum C_ℓ for multipoles ℓ = 2–10 is computed, and the sample variance of these nine values is taken. This scalar quantifies how unevenly power is distributed across the lowest multipoles. Under ΛCDM each C_ℓ is χ²(2ℓ+1)-distributed, so the expected variance is set by the fiducial spectrum. Both unusually high and low variance can signal anomalies such as suppressed low-ℓ power or oscillatory features in the Sachs–Wolfe plateau.
tests.hypothesis: The observed variance of C_ℓ for ℓ=2 to 10 is inconsistent with the distribution predicted by ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed for general exploration of anomalies in low-ℓ power dispersion, checking for significant deviations in both high and low variance directions. The code explicitly calculates two-sided p-values and interprets both high and low variance cases as potential anomalies, indicating no clear directional preference.
tests.results_text: Var(C_ℓ) = 5,300 μK⁴, well below the simulation mean of 90,377 ± 149,257 μK⁴. The observed value sits at the 1.7th percentile of 1,000 ΛCDM simulations, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.034 (z ≈ −0.57; the wide spread reflects the skewed distribution of the variance estimator). Anomaly strength is moderate.
tests.interpretation: The Planck low-ℓ power spectrum variance is notably low relative to ΛCDM expectations. The very low observed value suggests power among ℓ = 2–10 is more uniformly suppressed than typical simulations predict, consistent with the well-documented low quadrupole and overall low-ℓ power deficit. However, the two-tailed p = 0.034 falls just above the conventional 2σ threshold (p = 0.046 corresponds to 2σ for a Gaussian; here the non-Gaussianity of the test statistic complicates interpretation).
tests.literature: The low-ℓ power deficit, particularly the low quadrupole, is a long-standing anomaly noted since COBE and confirmed by WMAP and Planck (e.g., arXiv:2509.03719; Eriksen et al. 2004). However, testing the *variance* of C_ℓ across ℓ = 2–10 as a single scalar statistic is not a standard approach in the literature; most studies examine individual multipoles or ratios. No directly comparable published analysis was found.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Antipodal Temperature Difference Variance
tests.description: The CMB temperature map is filtered to retain only multipoles ℓ=2–5, and the temperature difference ΔT(θ,φ) = T(θ,φ) − T(π−θ, φ+π) is computed between each unmasked pixel and its antipode. The variance of ΔT over all valid pixels is taken as the test statistic. Since spherical harmonics satisfy Y_ℓm(π−θ,φ+π) = (−1)^ℓ Y_ℓm(θ,φ), even-ℓ modes contribute correlated signals while odd-ℓ modes contribute anti-correlated signals; the variance therefore probes the even–odd multipole balance.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map is consistent with isotropic ΛCDM simulations in terms of antipodal temperature difference variance at low multipoles.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any departure from isotropy at large angular scales, as evidenced by both high and low variance being of interest. The analysis code computes a two-sided p-value and interprets anomalies in both directions, indicating no prior directional preference.
tests.results_text: Planck Var(ΔT) = 1223.9 μK², compared to simulation mean 1480.8 ± 626.0 μK² (1000 ΛCDM realisations). The two-tailed p-value is 0.806 (z = −0.41, 40.3rd percentile). Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The observed antipodal temperature difference variance at low multipoles is fully consistent with isotropic ΛCDM simulations. There is no evidence of anomalous hemispherical power asymmetry or odd–even multipole imbalance in this pixel-space statistic.
tests.literature: Previous literature has studied hemispherical asymmetry and odd–even multipole imbalances using spherical-harmonic-space diagnostics (e.g., odd–even ℓ power ratios, dipole modulation). However, no directly comparable pixel-space variance-of-antipodal-differences statistic was identified in the search results or in prior work known to the author.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Map Roughness Heterogeneity
tests.description: The spherical Laplacian of the band-limited SMICA temperature map (ℓ = 2–30) is computed via the eigenvalue −ℓ(ℓ+1), and the sky is divided into ~60 equal-area azimuthal patches (10 iso-latitude rings with φ-sectors scaled by sin θ). The coefficient of variation (CV) of per-patch Laplacian variances quantifies how unevenly spatial "roughness" is distributed. An elevated CV would signal anisotropic temperature texture.
tests.hypothesis: The CV of per-patch Laplacian variance for the Planck map is significantly greater than expected from isotropic ΛCDM simulations, indicating spatially anisotropic temperature structure at low multipoles.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states that an "elevated CV" would indicate anisotropic temperature structure, and the code includes directional interpretation for an elevated CV as a potential anomaly. The motivation focuses on detecting "roughness heterogeneity" where some regions are systematically rougher or smoother—this is naturally a deviation toward higher CV, not lower. The alternative hypothesis is directional: we expect greater heterogeneity, not less.
tests.results_text: Planck CV = 0.4121. ΛCDM simulation mean = 0.4379 ± 0.0394 (1000 sims). The Planck value lies at the 25.3rd percentile (z ≈ −0.67σ). One-tailed upper p-value = 0.747; two-tailed p ≈ 0.506. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The Laplacian roughness distribution across the Planck sky is entirely consistent with isotropic Gaussian realizations. The observed CV is actually *slightly below* the simulation mean, opposite to the hypothesized excess. No evidence of spatially anisotropic temperature texture at low multipoles is found.
tests.literature: No prior study using per-patch Laplacian variance heterogeneity was found in the CMB literature. Related analyses include hemisphere-based power asymmetry tests (e.g., Eriksen et al. 2004; Planck 2020 XI) and local variance maps, but these operate on the temperature field directly rather than its Laplacian texture. The null result is consistent with the broader finding that low-ℓ spatial anomalies are localized to specific signatures (quadrupole–octopole alignment, cold spot) rather than global texture heterogeneity.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Spatial Autocorrelation Coefficient
tests.description: The Planck CMB temperature map is smoothed to ~10° FWHM to retain only large-scale structure. Moran's I spatial autocorrelation statistic is computed on the HEALPix grid using 8-neighbor adjacency, measuring whether neighboring pixels tend to have similar (positive I) or dissimilar (negative I) temperatures compared to isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM expectations.
tests.hypothesis: The Moran's I spatial autocorrelation coefficient of the Planck CMB temperature map deviates significantly from the distribution expected under isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect anomalies in both directions—positive autocorrelation indicating spatial clustering and negative autocorrelation indicating checkerboard-like structure—without a prior directional preference. The code explicitly computes a two-sided p-value and interprets deviations in either tail, supporting a non-directional hypothesis.
tests.results_text: Moran's I = 1.000753, compared to simulation mean = 1.001712 ± 0.000332. The Planck value falls at the 0.6th percentile of simulations, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.012 (z ≈ −2.89σ). Anomaly strength classified as strong.
tests.interpretation: Planck exhibits significantly negative spatial autocorrelation at large angular scales. The smoothed temperature map displays an unusually checkerboard-like pattern, with adjacent pixels tending to have opposite-sign temperatures more frequently than expected under ΛCDM. This suggests anti-correlated spatial structure or anomalous interference among low-ℓ spherical harmonic modes, consistent with previously noted large-angle anomalies.
tests.literature: No published studies were found applying Moran's I spatial autocorrelation specifically to CMB temperature maps. However, the finding is broadly consistent with known low-ℓ anomalies—including the quadrupole–octupole alignment, hemispherical power asymmetry, and cold spot—collectively suggesting departures from statistical isotropy at large angular scales. The checkerboard-like anti-correlation pattern detected here may relate to anomalous phase correlations among low-ℓ multipoles reported in other anomaly studies.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Temperature–Gradient-Magnitude Cross-Correlation Coefficient
tests.description: Compute the Pearson cross-correlation coefficient r between a band-limited (ℓ = 2–15) temperature map T and its gradient magnitude G = sqrt[(∂T/∂θ)² + (1/sin θ · ∂T/∂φ)²] over unmasked pixels. For a statistically isotropic Gaussian field, r is centered at zero by symmetry; significant nonzero values indicate spatially coherent non-Gaussianity where temperature extrema preferentially co-locate with regions of large or small gradient magnitude.
tests.hypothesis: The Pearson cross-correlation coefficient between the low-ℓ temperature map and its gradient magnitude is significantly different from zero.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any deviation from zero correlation, regardless of sign, as indicated by the two-sided p-value calculation in the summary function and the interpretation that checks for both significantly positive and negative anomalies. The description states that for a statistically isotropic Gaussian field, r is centered at zero by symmetry, and a significant nonzero value signals an anomaly, without specifying a directional preference. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck yields r = 0.047, compared to simulation mean 0.003 ± 0.078. The z-score is 0.55σ, the two-tailed p-value is 0.584, and Planck sits at the 70.8th percentile. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The observed cross-correlation between low-ℓ temperature and gradient magnitude is fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. Temperature hot/cold spots show no anomalous preference for co-locating with steep or flat gradient regions, indicating no detectable spatial coherence anomaly in the large-scale CMB temperature field.
tests.literature: No prior studies of cross-correlation between CMB temperature and gradient magnitude were found in the literature. Related low-ℓ anomaly tests include multipole vector alignments (Copi et al. 2004) and various non-Gaussianity probes, but this specific spatial derivative statistic does not appear to have been previously investigated.

tests.title: Temperature–Laplacian Cross-Correlation Coefficient
tests.description: Computes the Pearson correlation coefficient between the CMB temperature field T and its spherical Laplacian ∇²T at nside=256, using the harmonized Planck mask. The Laplacian is estimated via −ℓ(ℓ+1) multiplication in harmonic space. This scalar statistic probes whether temperature extrema show anomalous alignment with local spatial curvature, potentially sensitive to non-Gaussian clustering or atypical coherence patterns.
tests.hypothesis: The Pearson correlation coefficient between the CMB temperature field and its spherical Laplacian is zero, consistent with a statistically isotropic Gaussian field with no anomalous alignment between temperature extrema and local curvature.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, seeking any deviation from the expected null result regardless of sign. While the code computes both upper and lower p-values and interprets both positive and negative anomalies, there is no clear prior directional expectation from standard cosmological models—any nonzero correlation could signal atypical spatial coherence or non-Gaussian features in either direction.
tests.results_text: Planck r(T, ∇²T) = −0.5005, compared to simulation mean −0.4886 ± 0.0082. The Planck value falls at the 5.4th percentile of 1000 ΛCDM simulations, yielding z ≈ 1.61σ and a two-tailed p-value of 0.108.
tests.interpretation: The observed cross-correlation is consistent with the isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM expectation. Temperature extrema show no statistically significant anomalous relationship with local spatial curvature. The slight offset from the simulation mean (~1.6σ) falls well within normal statistical fluctuation and does not reach the 2σ anomaly threshold.
tests.literature: No prior studies using the Pearson correlation between T and ∇²T as a CMB anomaly statistic were identified in literature searches. Related work on CMB statistical anisotropy (e.g., Mukherjee & Souradeep 2015) addresses different statistics such as dipolar power asymmetry in harmonic space.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Band-Limited Cross-Correlation Coefficient
tests.description: The Pearson correlation coefficient is computed between two band-limited CMB temperature maps: a low-ℓ band (2≤ℓ≤10) and a high-ℓ band (11≤ℓ≤32). For a statistically isotropic Gaussian field, these multipole bands should be independent, so the expected correlation is zero. The test isolates pixel-space cross-correlation between large and intermediate angular scales within the low-multipole regime.
tests.hypothesis: The Pearson cross-correlation coefficient between low-ℓ (2≤ℓ≤10) and high-ℓ (11≤ℓ≤32) band-limited temperature maps is significantly different from zero, indicating anomalous cross-scale coherence inconsistent with a statistically isotropic Gaussian field.
tests.justification: The test description and code implementation explicitly compute a two-sided empirical p-value to assess deviations in both positive and negative directions from the expected zero correlation under isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM simulations. The interpretation in the code checks for both significant positive and negative anomalies, and there is no prior directional bias specified in the hypothesis; thus, a two-tailed test is appropriate for general exploration.
tests.results_text: Planck yields Pearson r = 0.0692, compared to simulations with mean 0.0386 ± 0.0083. The observed value sits at the 99.9th percentile of the simulation distribution (z ≈ 3.7σ), with a two-tailed p-value of 0.002. The anomaly strength is classified as strong.
tests.interpretation: Planck exhibits a significantly positive cross-correlation between the ℓ=2–10 and ℓ=11–32 band-limited temperature maps. Large-scale and intermediate-scale temperature features are unusually aligned, indicating coherent spatial structure across angular scales that should be independent under statistical isotropy. This cross-scale coherence may be related to known low-ℓ anomalies, such as the quadrupole–octupole alignment or the hemispherical asymmetry, suggesting a common origin in a possible departure from isotropy or Gaussianity.
tests.literature: Previous searches for direct studies of this specific band-limited cross-correlation statistic returned no clearly relevant results. The Planck 2018 VII isotropy paper (arXiv:1906.02552) surveys numerous low-ℓ anomalies but does not appear to include this exact test. Related work examines multipole alignments and phase correlations, but the pixel-space Pearson correlation between these specific ℓ bands is not a standard statistic in the anomaly literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Convex Hull Area Asymmetry
tests.description: The convex hull enclosing low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 20) hot spots (positive temperature pixels exceeding the 1σ threshold) is computed in 3D Cartesian space on the unit sphere. The ratio of its solid angle to 4π provides a single morphological scalar sensitive to whether hot regions are unusually clustered or elongated relative to isotropic Gaussian realisations.
tests.hypothesis: The convex hull area ratio of low-ℓ hot spots in the Planck CMB temperature map is consistent with the distribution expected from isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM simulations, with no significant morphological asymmetry or clustering.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect anomalies in the morphology of hot regions, which could manifest as either an unusually small convex hull area (indicating elongation or clustering) or an unusually large area (indicating dispersion). The analysis code computes a two-sided empirical p-value and interprets deviations in both directions, indicating no prior directional preference. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate for general exploration of potential anomalies.
tests.results_text: Planck's convex hull area ratio = 0.1450, compared to simulation mean = 0.1335 ± 0.0132. The value sits at the 80.5th percentile with a two-sided p-value of 0.39 (z ≈ 0.87σ). The anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The spatial extent of low-ℓ hot spots in the Planck map is fully consistent with random Gaussian realisations. There is no evidence for anomalous morphological clustering, elongation, or preferred directional compression of hot regions on the largest angular scales.
tests.literature: No prior studies applying convex hull morphology of low-ℓ CMB hot spots were identified in the search. Related morphological analyses of the CMB (e.g., genus statistics, Minkowski functionals) are well-established but do not employ this specific convex hull area ratio statistic.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Laplacian Skewness
tests.description: The angular Laplacian ∇²T is computed in harmonic space by multiplying each spherical harmonic coefficient T_{ℓm} (ℓ = 2–10) by −ℓ(ℓ+1), then synthesised to real space at nside=256. The skewness γ = ⟨(∇²T)³⟩ / ⟨(∇²T)²⟩^{3/2} measures asymmetry in the curvature distribution of the large-scale temperature pattern—e.g., whether cold spots have sharper curvature peaks than hot spots. This is distinct from raw temperature skewness, temperature–Laplacian cross-correlations, or gradient-magnitude statistics.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ (ℓ=2–10) Laplacian skewness of the observed CMB temperature map deviates from zero, indicating a non-Gaussian asymmetry in the spatial curvature distribution between hot and cold regions.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory and designed to detect any significant deviation from Gaussian expectations (γ=0) without a strong prior directional prediction. The code implements a two-sided empirical p-value and interprets both negative and positive skewness as potential anomalies, indicating a two-tailed framework is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck skewness = −0.142; simulation mean = −0.002 ± 0.144. The observed value lies at −0.97σ from the simulation mean (percentile 16.2%), yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.324. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ Laplacian skewness is fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. There is no evidence for asymmetric curvature structure between hot and cold regions at large angular scales. The result is unremarkable compared to the simulated ensemble.
