tests.literature: CMB non-Gaussianity at large scales has been probed extensively using other statistics—temperature skewness, Minkowski functionals, bispectrum estimators, and multipole vectors—but the specific statistic of Laplacian-field skewness does not appear to have been previously studied. The present search returned no relevant prior work on this quantity. The result is consistent with the general finding that Planck large-scale data show no strong evidence for primordial non-Gaussianity of generic type.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Two-Point Correlation Coherence Length
tests.description: The temperature map is band-limited to ℓ_max = 30, and the two-point correlation function C(θ) is computed in 1° bins from 0° to 180°, normalized by C(0). The statistic θ_half is the smallest angle where C(θ_half) ≤ 0.5, characterising how quickly spatial correlations decorrelate on large angular scales.
tests.hypothesis: The two-point correlation coherence length θ_half of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map from Planck data deviates from the predictions of the isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM model as estimated from simulations.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, designed to detect anomalies in large-scale temperature correlations in either direction—unusually coherent patterns (large θ_half) or unusually patchy structures (small θ_half). The analysis code computes a flat_ direction
tests.results_text: Planck yields θ_half = 7°, compared to a simulation mean of 8.5° ± 1.3°, corresponding to −1.11σ from the mean. The two-tailed p-value is 0.36 (percentile ≈ 0.0%). Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: Planck's coherence length is slightly shorter than the simulation average but well within the expected scatter. The two-point correlation function decorrelates at a rate fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM realisations. There is no evidence for unusually coherent large-scale patterns or unusually patchy low-ℓ structure.
tests.literature: The large-angle two-point correlation function has been extensively studied, beginning with Hinshaw et al. (1996) who noted a lack of correlations above ~60°, and later investigated in detail by Copi et al. (2007, 2009, 2010) and Slosar & Seljak (2004). Zhang (2012) [arXiv:1202.3469] examined directional dependence of the angular correlation function at low ℓ. While those works typically focused on the correlation function amplitude at large angles or its integrated absence, the present test uses a coherence-length metric (C(θ)/C(0) = 0.5 crossing angle), which is a different but closely related characterisation of the same underlying physics. The null result here is broadly consistent with analyses that find the low-ℓ two-point function anomaly depends on how the statistic is defined (e.g., Slosar & Seljak 2004 argued the significance is sensitive to sky cut choices).

tests.title: Hot-Cold Spot Count Asymmetry Ratio
tests.description: The number of local maxima (hot spots) and local minima (cold spots) are identified in the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 32) Planck temperature map using an 8-neighbor stencil on the HEALPix grid. The asymmetry ratio R = (N_hot − N_cold) / (N_hot + N_cold) quantifies the imbalance between hot and cold extrema. The statistic is compared against 1000 isotropic ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: There is an asymmetry between the number of hot and cold spots in the low-ℓ CMB temperature map (R ≠ 0), which may indicate a departure from isotropy or Gaussianity in the large-scale CMB.
tests.justification: The test statistic R measures the imbalance between hot and cold spots, and can be positive or negative. The code computes a two-sided p-value by considering both tails, and the interpretation handles deviations in either direction without a prior directional expectation. Since the anomaly could manifest as an excess of either hot or cold spots, a two-tailed test is appropriate for exploratory analysis.
tests.results_text: Planck yields R = −0.0065. The simulation mean is −0.0011 ± 0.0316, placing the observed value at −0.17σ from the mean (43.8th percentile). The two-tailed p-value is 0.88.
tests.interpretation: The hot and cold spot counts in the large-scale CMB are well-balanced, fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. The slight excess of cold spots over hot spots is negligible and well within the typical simulation scatter. No evidence for a preferred direction or handedness in the low-ℓ temperature pattern is found.
tests.literature: No directly comparable studies using this specific formal statistic were found. Related work includes CMB peak count statistics and studies of the CMB Cold Spot anomaly, but the formal asymmetry ratio between hot and cold local extrema at low multipoles does not appear to have been previously investigated as a dedicated anomaly test.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Largest Hot Region Angular Size
tests.description: Threshold the band-limited (ℓ=2–10) CMB temperature map at its mean to identify hot pixels. Using 4-connectivity on the nside=256 HEALPix grid, find connected components of hot pixels and compute the area of each. The statistic is the angular diameter (= 2√(area/π)) of the largest connected hot region.
tests.hypothesis: The angular diameter of the largest connected hot region in the low-ℓ CMB temperature map is consistent with the distribution predicted by the isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test description and code do not specify a directional preference; the summarize function explicitly computes a two-sided empirical p-value by considering both upper and lower tails, and the interpretation examines deviations in both directions (unusually large or small). This approach is standard for exploratory anomaly tests where no prior directional hypothesis exists, allowing detection of any departure from the ΛCDM expectations.
tests.results_text: Planck's largest hot region diameter = 102.50°, compared to a simulation mean of 100.12° ± 8.80° (z = +0.27σ). Two-tailed empirical p-value = 0.902, placing Planck at the 54.9th percentile. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The angular extent of the largest connected hot region in the low-ℓ CMB temperature field is fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. The observed value sits comfortably within the bulk of the simulated distribution, showing no tendency toward unusually large or fragmented hot structures at large angular scales.
tests.literature: Studies of CMB spot morphology at large scales have examined ellipticity (Gurzadyan et al. 2003) and hot/cold spot spatial uniformity via orientation matrices (Khan & Saha 2022). Abramo et al. (2006) discussed extended foregrounds linked to low-ℓ anomalies. However, none of these specifically quantify the angular diameter of the largest connected hot component using graph-based connected-component analysis. The specific statistic tested here does not appear to have a direct precedent in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Peak-to-RMS Temperature Ratio
tests.description: Band-limit the CMB temperature map to ℓ ≤ 20 via an alm cutoff. Compute the RMS temperature and the maximum absolute temperature over unmasked pixels. The statistic is max|T| / RMS, which for a Gaussian random field has a well-defined expectation (~3–4). Deviations would signal anomalous concentration of extreme temperature features at large angular scales.
tests.hypothesis: The peak-to-RMS temperature ratio for the CMB at ℓ ≤ 20 is consistent with the distribution predicted by isotropic Gaussian LCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, checking for any significant deviation from the expected Gaussian random field behavior, as indicated by the two-sided p-value calculation in the code and the description's focus on detecting values "far from the LCDM-simulated distribution" without specifying a direction (e.g., only higher or lower).
tests.results_text: Observed peak-to-RMS ratio = 3.52. LCDM simulation mean = 3.48 ± 0.36, yielding z = +0.11σ. Two-tailed p = 0.80 (percentile = 60%). Anomaly strength: negligible.
tests.interpretation: The peak-to-RMS ratio at low multipoles is fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian LCDM expectations. The most extreme temperature features at ℓ ≤ 20 are neither unusually large nor unusually small relative to the overall RMS fluctuation, indicating no anomalous clustering of hot or cold spots at the largest angular scales.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ CMB anomalies are extensively studied, including the quadrupole–octupole alignment (Tegmark et al., 2003; de Oliveira-Costa et al., 2004), the power deficit at ℓ ≲ 30 (Planck Collaboration XXIII, 2014), and the CMB Cold Spot (Cruz et al., 2005; Vielva et al., 2004). However, the specific peak-to-RMS ratio as a test statistic is not a standard probe in the anomaly literature; related work typically focuses on angular power spectrum anomalies, multipole alignments, or non-Gaussian signatures via Minkowski functionals or needlet analyses (e.g., Planck 2018 results VII).

tests.title: Low-ℓ Extrema Ellipticity
tests.description: The mean ellipticity of temperature extrema is computed in the SMICA map band-limited to ℓ ≤ 32. Local maxima and minima are identified via nearest-neighbour comparison on the HEALPix grid. For each extremum, the Hessian matrix is estimated by fitting a quadratic surface to the 9-point neighbourhood, yielding eigenvalues λ₁ ≥ λ₂. The ellipticity is e = (|λ₁|−|λ₂|)/(|λ₁|+|λ₂|), averaged over all extrema to produce a single scalar quantifying mean elongation of large-scale CMB features.
tests.hypothesis: The mean ellipticity of low-ℓ temperature extrema in the Planck CMB map is inconsistent with the distribution expected from isotropic Gaussian LCDM simulations, indicating either unusually elongated or unusually round extrema.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect unexpected morphological coherence or isotropy by measuring the elongation of extrema. The anomaly could manifest as either unusually high ellipticity (elongated features) or unusually low ellipticity (round features), as both could indicate deviations from statistical isotropy. The analysis code computes a two-sided p-value and interprets deviations in both directions, confirming the exploratory nature of the test without a prior directional preference.
tests.results_text: Planck mean ellipticity = 0.4190. Simulation mean = 0.4015 ± 0.0152, giving a deviation of +1.15σ. Two-tailed p-value = 0.222, percentile = 88.9%. Anomaly strength is negligible; the Planck value lies comfortably within the simulation distribution.
tests.interpretation: The ellipticity of low-ℓ extrema in the Planck SMICA map is fully consistent with isotropic Gaussian LCDM expectations. There is no evidence for unusually elongated or round temperature features at large angular scales. The ~1σ upward shift is well within statistical fluctuations from 1000 simulations and does not suggest any morphological anomaly.
tests.literature: No directly comparable studies of Hessian-derived extrema ellipticity in the low-ℓ CMB were found in the literature. Related morphological analyses exist (e.g., Minkowski functionals, extrema counts), but this specific statistic appears not to have been previously investigated.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Genus Density
tests.description: Computes the topological asymmetry between hot and cold connected regions in the CMB temperature map at a single zero-temperature threshold, defined as genus density G/(4π) = (n_hot − n_cold)/(4π). The map is band-limited to ℓ ≤ 32. This compact scalar summary is sensitive to non-Gaussian features distinct from peak-count or moment-based tests.
tests.hypothesis: The genus density (n_hot - n_cold)/(4π) of the CMB temperature map at large angular scales (ℓ ≤ 32) deviates from the expected distribution under the standard ΛCDM Gaussian isotropic model.
tests.justification: The test statistic is fundamentally symmetric—it can be positive (more hot regions) or negative (more cold regions)—and the anomaly could plausibly manifest in either direction (e.g., an excess of hot or cold spots). The analysis code explicitly computes a two-sided p‑value (taking twice the minimum of the upper and lower tail probabilities), and there is no stated prior expectation for a specific direction in the description. Thus, the test is exploratory, checking for any deviation without directional preference.
tests.results_text: The Planck genus density is 0.637, while 1000 ΛCDM simulations yield a mean of 0.010 ± 0.770. The Planck value falls at the 78.1st percentile (z = +0.81σ), yielding a two-sided p-value of 0.438. Anomaly strength: negligible.
tests.interpretation: The asymmetry between hot and cold connected regions in the low-ℓ CMB is fully consistent with Gaussian isotropic ΛCDM expectations. There is no indication of topological excess in either hot or cold connected components at large angular scales.
tests.literature: The genus statistic is well-established in CMB topology studies (e.g., Gott, Melott & Dickinson 1986; Park et al. 2001; Ducos et al. 2020), typically computed at multiple threshold levels to reconstruct the full genus curve across the temperature distribution. However, no published studies were found specifically applying a single zero-threshold genus density to low-ℓ CMB maps. The search returned no directly relevant CMB genus results, suggesting this particular formulation has limited prior coverage.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Wavelet Entropy Coefficient
tests.description: A discrete wavelet transform decomposes the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 32) masked CMB temperature map across J_max = log2(nside) scales. At each scale j, Shannon entropy H_j is computed from the histogram of |wavelet coefficients|. These entropies are averaged into a single scalar capturing multi-scale non-Gaussianity and information content in the large-scale wavelet domain.
tests.hypothesis: The mean wavelet entropy of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map differs from the distribution expected under the standard ΛCDM cosmological model.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to capture "multi-scale non-Gaussianity and information content," with the analysis code calculating and interpreting anomalies in both directions (higher or lower entropy than simulations). The `summarize_results` function computes both an upper and lower p-value and selects the smaller for the two-sided p-value. The interpretation strings describe different physical scenarios for both significantly higher and lower entropy. This indicates a general exploration for any unexpected pattern in the wavelet entropy statistic, rather than a prediction of a specific directional change, making a two-tailed test appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck mean wavelet entropy = 5.985 bits. Simulation mean = 6.045 ± 0.054 bits. The Planck value lies at the 12.5th percentile, deviating −1.12σ from simulations. Two-sided p-value = 0.250, anomaly strength classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The mean wavelet entropy of the low-ℓ Planck CMB map is fully consistent with Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. The mild −1.12σ deficit suggests no significant departure in the distribution of multi-scale wavelet coefficient structure. There is no indication of concentrated or disordered large-scale wavelet patterns beyond what isotropic Gaussian fluctuations predict.
tests.literature: Wavelet-based CMB analyses are well established (e.g., Vielva et al. 2004; Planck 2018 VII), but a specific wavelet entropy coefficient restricted to low multipoles does not appear as a standard statistic in the anomaly literature. Searches returned no directly comparable studies combining Shannon entropy of wavelet coefficients with low-ℓ masking. This may represent a novel construction, though foundational wavelet techniques are common.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Angular Pseudoentropy
tests.description: Compute the angular pseudoentropy S_angular = -∫ ρ(θ,φ) ln ρ(θ,φ) dΩ of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 10) CMB temperature field, where ρ is the normalised absolute temperature distribution over the sphere. This rotationally invariant scalar quantifies spatial uniformity of low-ℓ power; deviations from the characteristic Gaussian value signal non-Gaussianity or anisotropy. Compared against 1000 Gaussian isotropic LCDM simulations at nside=256.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ angular pseudoentropy of the observed CMB map deviates from the distribution predicted by Gaussian isotropic LCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory without a prior directional prediction; the analysis code calculates two-sided p-values, and the interpretation considers both higher and lower entropy as potential anomalies.
tests.results_text: Observed S = 2.231 nats vs. simulation mean 2.212 ± 0.024 nats (z = +0.76σ). Percentile = 77.6%, two-tailed p = 0.448. Anomaly strength: negligible. The maximum possible entropy (uniform sphere) is 2.531 nats.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ angular pseudoentropy lies well within the distribution expected from Gaussian isotropic simulations. No evidence for anomalous non-uniformity or concentration of low-ℓ temperature power on the sphere. This is consistent with the absence of statistically significant large-scale anisotropy in the temperature field.
tests.literature: The concept of pseudoentropy for CMB analysis was introduced and applied to Planck 2015 data by Pinkwart, Schupp & Schwarz (2019), who linked it to multipole vectors for detecting statistical anisotropy. The present test focuses specifically on low multipoles using the normalised absolute temperature field, rather than the full multipole structure studied previously. No direct prior application of this exact statistic to ℓ ≤ 10 was identified, though the underlying entropy framework is established.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Threshold-Contour Perimeter Coefficient of Variation
tests.description: A morphological statistic measuring the coefficient of variation (CV) of iso-temperature contour perimeter lengths across nine percentile thresholds (10th–90th) of the low-ℓ (ℓ=2–30) CMB temperature field. Boundary pixels are identified at each threshold as hot pixels with at least one cold or masked neighbor, and perimeters are scaled by average pixel diameter at nside=256. Higher CV indicates uneven distribution of morphological complexity across temperature levels, which can signal non-Gaussian features such as cold spots or dipole modulation.
tests.hypothesis: The coefficient of variation of iso-temperature contour perimeters for the Planck CMB map differs from the distribution expected under the standard ΛCDM Gaussian random field model.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any deviation in morphological complexity distribution from Gaussian expectations. The anomaly could manifest as either higher CV (uneven complexity, e.g., from a cold spot) or lower CV (unusually uniform complexity). The summary function explicitly computes a two-sided p-value and interprets both higher and lower directions, indicating a general exploratory test for any significant change.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA map yields CV = 0.2447, compared to the simulation mean of 0.2646 ± 0.0176 (−1.13σ, percentile = 13.0%). The two-sided p-value is 0.26, indicating no significant departure from Gaussian LCDM expectations. Anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The contour perimeter variability across temperature thresholds is fully consistent with the standard ΛCDM Gaussian random field model. The Planck value sits slightly below the simulation mean but well within the expected scatter, showing no evidence that morphological complexity is preferentially concentrated at particular temperature levels (e.g., due to a cold spot or modulation pattern).
tests.literature: No directly comparable studies using a coefficient of variation of iso-temperature contour perimeters were found. Related morphological analyses include Minkowski functionals, genus curves, and spot ellipticity studies (e.g., Gurzadyan et al. 2003), but this specific statistic appears unexplored in the CMB anomaly literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Local Texture Diversity Entropy
tests.description: The masked Planck temperature map is band-pass filtered to multipoles 2 ≤ ℓ ≤ 32 and downgraded to nside=32. For each unmasked pixel, an 8-bit Local Binary Pattern (LBP) code is computed from the 8 HEALPix neighbors. A normalized histogram of 256 possible codes is constructed, and its Shannon entropy H = −Σ pᵢ log₂(pᵢ) serves as the anomaly statistic, quantifying diversity and uniformity of local texture patterns in the low-ℓ CMB field.
tests.hypothesis: The Shannon entropy of the 8-bit LBP code distribution in the low-ℓ (ℓ=2–32) CMB temperature map deviates from the mean expected under standard ΛCDM simulations, indicating anomalous local texture diversity.
tests.justification: The test description and implementation do not specify a directional expectation (e.g., predicting only higher or only lower entropy). The anomaly statistic measures "diversity and uniformity," which could deviate in either direction—excess entropy (unusually diverse textures) or deficit entropy (unusually homogeneous textures). The summarize_results function explicitly computes both upper and lower p-values and combines them into a two-sided p-value, confirming the two-tailed nature. Since no prior physical or observational evidence points specifically to one direction, a two-tailed test is appropriate for general anomaly detection.
tests.results_text: Planck LBP entropy = 5.796 bits (72.4% of the theoretical maximum 8.0). Simulation mean = 5.745 ± 0.028 bits. The Planck value falls at the 95.8th percentile, yielding z ≈ 1.82σ and a two-tailed p-value of 0.084. Anomaly strength is classified as moderate.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ Planck map shows moderately elevated LBP texture diversity compared to ΛCDM simulations, suggesting slightly richer local pattern configurations than expected. However, the deviation does not reach conventional statistical significance (2σ / p < 0.05). This could hint at subtle large-scale spatial organization (e.g., hemispherical asymmetry or localized features) but is consistent with Gaussian fluctuations at marginal levels.
tests.literature: No prior studies applying Local Binary Pattern texture analysis or Shannon entropy of LBP code distributions to CMB anomaly testing were identified. LBP is a well-established tool in computer vision and texture analysis but its use in CMB statistical isotropy studies appears unexplored in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Sublevel-Set Persistence Lifetime Skewness
tests.description: Band-pass filter the Planck CMB temperature map to retain multipoles 2 ≤ ℓ ≤ 30, then apply a sublevel-set filtration on HEALPix pixel centres, tracking connected components as the temperature threshold rises. Each component's persistence lifetime (death − birth temperature) quantifies its topological significance. The skewness of positive lifetimes captures whether the low-ℓ field is dominated by many short-lived features (negative skewness) or a few very persistent large-scale structures (positive skewness).
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map has a persistence lifetime skewness statistic that differs from the mean of the LCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to explore any significant deviation in the skewness statistic, which can be either positive (indicating dominance of a few persistent large-scale features) or negative (indicating dominance of many short-lived features). The analysis code explicitly checks for both directions by computing a two-sided p-value and interpreting anomaly strength for both high and low values. There is no strong prior directional prediction stated; the test is general exploration for an anomaly. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck persistence lifetime skewness = 1.631. Simulation mean = 1.585 ± 0.264. The Planck value deviates by only +0.17σ from the LCDM simulation mean, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.788. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The persistence lifetime skewness of the Planck low-ℓ map is fully consistent with Gaussian LCDM expectations. There is no evidence that large-scale topological features (e.g., the cold spot, hemispherical asymmetry) produce an unusual excess of persistent connected components relative to the simulation ensemble.
tests.literature: Prior online searches for persistent homology applied to CMB anomaly detection returned no directly relevant studies. The general statistical theory of persistent homology has been explored (e.g., Bubenik & Kim 2007), but application of persistence lifetime skewness to CMB maps as an anomaly test does not appear in the literature to my knowledge.

tests.title: Low-ℓ T² Asphericity Eigenvalue Ratio
tests.description: A weighted inertia tensor I_{ij} is computed using T(p)² as weights over pixels in the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 30) masked CMB map. After diagonalizing the 3×3 tensor to obtain eigenvalues λ₁ ≥ λ₂ ≥ λ₃ (summing to zero), the asphericity ratio A = (λ₁ − λ₃)/λ₁ is computed to quantify the elongation of the squared temperature pattern. The Planck SMICA map value is compared to distributions from 1000 ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The asphericity ratio A of the squared temperature pattern in the Planck low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 30) map is consistent with the distribution predicted by isotropic, Gaussian ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, checking for any significant deviation from ΛCDM expectations in the asphericity of the squared temperature pattern—either more elongated/flattened than expected or more spherical. The implementation computes both upper and lower p-values and combines them into a two-sided p-value, confirming the two-tailed nature. No prior directional hypothesis is specified in the test description or code; it's a general check for anomalies.
tests.results_text: Planck asphericity ratio A = 2.386 compared to simulation mean = 1.889 ± 0.250, giving a deviation of +1.99σ (percentile = 94.5%). Two-sided p-value = 0.11.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ squared temperature pattern shows marginally higher asphericity than typical ΛCDM realizations, but the deviation does not reach the 2σ threshold. The result falls within expectations for a Gaussian, isotropic sky. The specific physical interpretation—whether prolate or oblate elongation—depends on the eigenvalue structure, but statistically no significant departure from ΛCDM is detected.
tests.literature: Standard low-ℓ CMB anomaly studies (e.g., Planck Collaboration VII, 2020) examine quadrupole–octupole alignment, parity asymmetry, and hemispherical power asymmetry rather than T²-weighted asphericity statistics. No directly comparable studies using this specific T²-weighted inertia tensor approach were identified in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Multifractal Scaling Width
tests.description: The width of the multifractal singularity spectrum ΔD = D(−2) − D(2) is computed for the Planck CMB temperature map band-limited to ℓ ≤ 30. The sky is partitioned into equal-area HEALPix nested patches at five scales (~2°–32° diameter). Rényi dimensions D(q) for q ∈ {−2, −1, 0, 1, 2} are estimated via linear regression of qth-order Rényi entropy against log(patch size). ΔD quantifies the range of scaling exponents: ≈0 for uniform Gaussian fields, larger for heterogeneous spatial structure.
tests.hypothesis: The multifractal spectrum width ΔD = D(−2) − D(2) of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map deviates significantly from the distribution predicted by ΛCDM simulations, indicating an anomaly in spatial heterogeneity.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to detect deviations in both directions, as evidenced by the code calculating two-sided p-values and interpreting anomalies for both wider (larger ΔD) and narrower (smaller ΔD) spectra relative to simulations. The description of ΔD highlights that positive values indicate heterogeneity, but this is a descriptive measure rather than a directional prediction; the anomaly test is exploratory for any departure from the expected uniform Gaussian field, necessitating a two-tailed approach.
tests.results_text: Planck ΔD = 0.6856, compared to ΛCDM simulation mean 0.6528 ± 0.0284. The Planck value lies at +1.15σ (percentile 90.2%), yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.196. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The multifractal spectrum width of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map is fully consistent with ΛCDM expectations. There is no indication of anomalous spatial heterogeneity or homogeneity at large angular scales compared to isotropic Gaussian realisations.
tests.literature: Multifractal analysis has been applied to cosmological structures (e.g., galaxy clustering, cosmic web), but accessible literature does not appear to contain a direct study of Rényi-dimension-based singularity spectrum width applied specifically to the low-ℓ CMB temperature field. This limits contextualisation with prior work.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Average Bispectrum Amplitude
tests.description: For the Planck CMB temperature map limited to ℓ ≤ 30, compute the mean absolute bispectrum ⟨|B|⟩ over all valid mode triples (satisfying triangle and parity conditions) to quantify average phase coupling (non-Gaussianity) among low-ℓ modes.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ average bispectrum amplitude of the Planck CMB temperature map is consistent with the expected distribution from ΛCDM Gaussian simulations, indicating no significant anomaly in phase coupling among low-ℓ modes.
tests.justification: The test description notes that a higher average bispectrum amplitude suggests stronger departure from Gaussianity, but the implementation in the code considers both directions of deviation. Specifically, the `summarize_results` function calculates two-sided p-values by comparing the Planck statistic to both higher and lower extremes of the simulation distribution, and provides interpretations for cases where the statistic is significantly higher or lower. This approach is appropriate for general exploration without a strong prior directional prediction, making the test two-tailed.
tests.results_text: Planck ⟨|B|⟩ = 4580.76 μK³. ΛCDM Gaussian simulations (1000 runs): mean 8231.45 ± 6708.49 μK³, yielding a z-score of -0.54σ and a two-tailed p-value of 0.748.
tests.interpretation: The Planck statistic is consistent with the simulated distribution, indicating no significant departure from Gaussian expectations for low-ℓ phase coupling.
tests.literature: Previous web searches did not find direct references to this exact statistic; bispectrum analyses in CMB literature (e.g., lensing bispectrum studies) are common, but low-ℓ average amplitude is not a standard metric.
tests.novelty_raw: Variation (a specific scalar derived from bispectrum analysis, not a well-documented standalone statistic)

tests.title: Low-ℓ Laplacian Spatial Correlation Amplitude
tests.description: Compute the Laplacian (∇²T) of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map (ℓ ≤ 30) on an nside=256 sphere. The normalized two-point spatial correlation function |C(θ)| of this Laplacian field is calculated for angular separations 10°–170° in 5° steps and fitted with an exponential decay model A·exp(−θ/ξ), where A is the amplitude at θ=10° and ξ the coherence length. The scalar test statistic is the fitted amplitude A.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits an anomaly in the low-ℓ Laplacian spatial correlation amplitude, as indicated by the fitted amplitude A being significantly different from the distribution expected from ΛCDM Gaussian simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed for general exploration of anomalies in the Laplacian correlation amplitude without a predefined directional preference. The analysis code explicitly computes a two-sided p-value by taking the minimum of the upper and lower tail probabilities and scaling by two, confirming that both higher and lower values of A are considered potential anomalies. This approach aligns with a two-tailed hypothesis test.
tests.results_text: Planck amplitude A = 0.2244 lies at −1.00σ from the ΛCDM simulation mean (0.2311 ± 0.0067). Two-tailed p-value = 0.302 (percentile = 15.1%). Anomaly strength classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The Laplacian spatial correlation amplitude in the Planck data is fully consistent with Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. The approximately one-sigma deficit is statistically unremarkable and does not indicate any anomalous spatial structure in the low-ℓ temperature gradient field.
tests.literature: The CMB low-ℓ regime has been extensively studied for anomalies such as quadrupole–octupole alignment, power suppression, and hemispherical asymmetries. The Laplacian of the temperature field amplifies small-scale gradient features but has not been commonly used as a correlation statistic. While related work exists on low-ℓ ellipticity (Gurzadyan et al. 2003) and extended foreground effects (Abramo et al. 2006), no directly comparable Laplacian correlation amplitude analysis was found in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gaussian Curvature Skewness
tests.description: Compute the Gaussian curvature K = T_θθ·T_φφ − (T_θφ)² of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 20) SMICA temperature surface at each pixel (nside=256) via finite-difference Hessian approximations, then measure the spatial skewness γ_K of K across unmasked pixels. For a Gaussian random field, the distribution of K is symmetric (γ_K ≈ 0); significant skewness indicates preferential concentration of elliptic (peak/valley) versus saddle-point regions, probing geometric non-Gaussianity in contour topology.
tests.hypothesis: The skewness of the Gaussian curvature K of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 20) CMB temperature field is significantly different from zero, indicating a departure from the symmetric distribution expected for a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any significant deviation (positive or negative) from the null expectation of zero skewness, as stated in the description: "For a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field, the distribution of K is symmetric and γ_K ≈ 0; a significant departure indicates preferential concentration of either convex/concave structure or saddle-like structure." There is no prior directional hypothesis (e.g., expecting an excess of peaks over saddle points); it is a general geometric non-Gaussianity probe. The code also computes a two-sided p-value, confirming the exploratory, two-tailed nature.
tests.results_text: Planck γ_K = −60.18. Simulation mean = −98.02 ± 74.19 (z = +0.51σ). Percentile = 86.3%, two-tailed p = 0.274. Anomaly strength: negligible.
tests.interpretation: The Gaussian curvature skewness of the low-ℓ temperature field is fully consistent with ΛCDM Gaussian expectations. The balance between elliptic and saddle-point regions shows no significant anomaly in the geometric structure of temperature contours. The result lies well within the simulation envelope.
tests.literature: Barreiro et al. (2000, astro-ph/0004218) studied the Gaussian curvature of CMB excursion sets as a non-Gaussianity estimator and found it effective for detecting perturbations with skewness/kurtosis. However, that work examined the topology of excursion set surfaces at specific thresholds, whereas this test measures the skewness of the pixel-by-pixel Gaussian curvature of the temperature field itself—a related but distinct statistic. No prior studies using this specific skewness-of-K approach were identified in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Skewness
tests.description: The spatial gradient magnitude field of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 32) CMB temperature map is computed using spherical harmonic derivatives. The skewness (third standardized moment) of the gradient magnitude values across all unmasked pixels is then calculated, capturing asymmetries in the sharpness and distribution of temperature gradients and potentially revealing non-Gaussian features in the large-scale temperature topology.
tests.hypothesis: The skewness of the gradient magnitude of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map is consistent with the distribution of skewness values from ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to explore non-Gaussian features in the low-ℓ temperature field by measuring the skewness of the gradient magnitude. There is no a priori reason to expect the skewness to be specifically higher or lower than the ΛCDM expectation; the anomaly could manifest as either too high or too low skewness. The code computes a two-sided p-value, confirming that deviations in both directions are of interest.
tests.results_text: The observed skewness of |∇T| is 0.5885, compared to a simulation mean of 0.6453 ± 0.0634. This places Planck at the 19.0th percentile, corresponding to a deviation of −0.88σ. The two-sided p-value is 0.38, well above the 0.05 threshold, indicating no significant tension with ΛCDM predictions. The anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The gradient magnitude skewness is fully consistent with ΛCDM Gaussian expectations. The distribution of temperature gradients shows no significant asymmetry beyond what is expected from a statistically isotropic random field. There is no indication of excess localized sharp features, anomalous structures, or hemispherical asymmetries in the large-scale CMB gradient topology. The mild 19th-percentile positioning is well within normal statistical fluctuation.
tests.literature: No prior studies specifically measuring the skewness of the CMB gradient magnitude field at low multipoles were identified in the search. Related low-ℓ anomaly tests exist—such as multipole vector correlations (Copi et al. 2004) and spot ellipticity measurements (Gurzadyan et al. 2003)—but these use fundamentally different statistics. The gradient magnitude skewness probes a distinct aspect of the temperature field topology.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Squared-Temperature Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: Computes T² on the celestial sphere, subtracts its mean, then extracts the rms dipole amplitude D = sqrt(|a_{1,-1}|² + |a_{1,0}|² + |a_{1,1}|²) from the ℓ=1 spherical harmonic coefficients. This probes dipolar non-Gaussianity in the squared temperature field, distinct from direct temperature dipole modulations.
tests.hypothesis: The squared temperature dipole amplitude D in the Planck CMB map is larger than expected from Gaussian ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect an unusually large dipole amplitude in the squared temperature field, as indicated by the p-value computation (sim >= planck_stat) and the interpretation in the code which focuses on an unusually large dipole amplitude. There is a clear directional prediction for an excess.
tests.results_text: D = 1911 μK² in Planck, compared to FFP simulation mean of 1342 ± 677 μK². Planck sits at the 81.4th percentile (+0.84σ), with one-sided p = 0.186 across 1000 simulations. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The squared-temperature dipole amplitude is fully consistent with Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. No significant preferred direction in T² is detected, indicating no evidence for the specific non-Gaussian coupling that an anomalous T² dipole would signal.
tests.literature: The T² dipole statistic is not a standard probe in the CMB anomalies literature. Related work includes dipolar modulation tests (e.g., Eriksen et al. 2004; Hoftuft et al. 2009), multipole vector analyses (Copi, Huterer & Starkman 2004), and various low-ℓ alignment studies, but none of these specifically extract the dipole of the squared temperature field. Searches for direct prior use of this particular statistic returned no relevant results, so it appears to occupy an unexplored niche.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Maximum Ecliptic Latitude
tests.description: Computes gradient magnitude of the low-ℓ (ℓ=2–10) CMB temperature map via spherical harmonic derivatives. Identifies the pixel with maximum gradient and converts its position to ecliptic coordinates. The statistic is |sin(ecliptic latitude)|, designed to be small if the steepest temperature change aligns with the ecliptic plane.
tests.hypothesis: The maximum gradient in the low-ℓ (ℓ=2–10) CMB temperature map is significantly aligned with the ecliptic plane, resulting in a lower-than-expected |sin(ecliptic latitude)| statistic.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly focuses on measuring how close the steepest temperature change is to the ecliptic plane, with the statistic |sin(ecliptic latitude)| designed to be small when aligned with the plane. The code interpretation section highlights that small values indicate alignment with the ecliptic plane and the analysis computes a specific one-sided p-value for the ecliptic plane alignment. This represents a clear directional hypothesis about an anomaly, rather than a general deviation in any direction.
tests.results_text: Planck yields |sin(ecl_lat)| = 0.9184 (ecliptic lat ≈ 66.7°), placing the gradient maximum near the ecliptic pole rather than the plane. Simulations give mean = 0.9175 ± 0.0009. The Planck value is +0.96σ above the simulation mean (percentile = 76.7%), with two-sided p = 0.466. One-sided p-value toward the ecliptic plane = 1.0, meaning zero simulations produced a smaller value. Anomaly strength is negligible.
tests.interpretation: The maximum gradient in the low-ℓ temperature field lies close to the ecliptic pole, not the ecliptic plane. This result is fully consistent with Gaussian random realizations. No evidence is found for ecliptic-plane alignment of the large-scale temperature gradient structure.
tests.literature: No directly comparable study using gradient-based statistics was found in the search results. Related work by Weeks (2004) and Abramo et al. (2006) examined multipole vector alignment with the ecliptic plane at low ℓ, finding some evidence for quadrupole–octopole–ecliptic alignment at >2σ. The present gradient-based approach probes a different observable and finds no such anomaly.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Hottest Spot Galactic Latitude
tests.description: The test identifies the Galactic latitude of the pixel with the maximum temperature in the CMB map smoothed to ℓ_max ≤ 10. It checks if this hottest spot is preferentially located closer to the Galactic plane than expected in ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The hottest spot in the low-ℓ (ℓ≤10) smoothed CMB temperature map is located closer to the Galactic plane than expected from Gaussian simulations.
tests.justification: The test is specifically designed to check for Galactic plane concentration, as indicated by the description focusing on proximity to the Galactic plane and the code computing p-value for smaller absolute Galactic latitude (p(|b| ≤ observed)). The interpretation in the code explicitly mentions that a small p-value suggests the hottest spot is unusually close to the Galactic plane, indicating a directional prediction that the observed absolute latitude will be smaller than expected from simulations.
tests.results_text: The hottest spot in the Planck data is at a Galactic latitude of -55.1°. From 1000 Gaussian simulations, the mean latitude is 0.5° ± 44.5°. The observed value is 1.25σ from the mean. The one-tailed p-value (for a spot closer to the plane) is 0.783, indicating no significant concentration toward the Galactic plane.
tests.interpretation: The observed hottest spot is at a mid-latitude in the southern Galactic hemisphere. Its location is statistically consistent with the distribution expected from Gaussian, isotropic realizations of the ΛCDM model. There is no evidence for an anomalous Galactic plane alignment.
tests.literature: This specific test (hottest spot latitude in low-ℓ) is not commonly reported, but related analyses of low-ℓ multipole alignment with the Galactic plane or "axis of evil" are standard in CMB anomaly studies (e.g., Land & Magueijo, 2005). This null result is consistent with some studies finding no strong alignment for the hottest spot.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Kurtosis Ratio
tests.description: Computes the excess kurtosis of the gradient magnitude |∇T| from the CMB temperature map band-limited to multipoles ℓ ∈ [2, 20]. This statistic quantifies the "tail-heaviness" of gradient values, indicating whether sharp temperature features are concentrated in specific regions.
tests.hypothesis: The observed excess kurtosis of the gradient magnitude in the low-ℓ CMB map is significantly different from the distribution expected from Gaussian LCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect both anomalously high kurtosis (indicating concentrated sharp features) and anomalously low kurtosis (indicating unusually uniform textures). The code computes both one-sided p-values and combines them into a two-sided p-value, indicating no directional preference. Since we are interested in any significant deviation from the LCDM expectations, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: The Planck SMICA map has an excess kurtosis of 0.5825. This value is +0.99σ away from the mean of 1000 ΛCDM simulations (0.2443 ± 0.3407), placing it at the 86.6th percentile. The two-tailed p-value is 0.268.
tests.interpretation: The observed kurtosis is consistent with the distribution expected from a Gaussian ΛCDM universe. There is no statistically significant evidence for an unusual concentration of sharp temperature features (e.g., hot or cold spots) in the low-ℓ CMB texture.
tests.literature: The specific statistic of gradient magnitude kurtosis at low-ℓ does not appear to be a standard test in the CMB anomaly literature. Related studies examine other low-ℓ features like alignment or planarity of multipoles, but not this gradient-based metric.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Saddle-Point Mean Eccentricity
tests.description: The SMICA temperature map is band-limited to ℓ ≤ 30 and saddle points are identified as pixels where the Hessian has negative determinant. At each saddle, the Hessian is diagonalised and an eccentricity e = 1 − |λ₂|/|λ₁| is computed (0 = circular, 1 = maximally elongated). The mean eccentricity over all saddles is compared against 1000 ΛCDM simulations to test whether real saddle points show anomalous elongation or compression.
tests.hypothesis: The mean eccentricity of saddle points in the low-ℓ (ℓ≤30) CMB temperature map is consistent with the distribution from LCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test assesses whether saddle points are anomalously elongated or compressed, indicating a two-tailed investigation. The code computes both upper and lower p-values and the interpretation considers deviations in both directions. There is no stated directional preference.
tests.results_text: Observed mean eccentricity = 0.5768. Simulation mean = 0.5696 ± 0.0044, yielding z = +1.62σ (94.2nd percentile, two-sided p = 0.116). No strong departure from the ΛCDM distribution is present.
tests.interpretation: The observed saddle-point eccentricity sits comfortably within the simulation envelope. At +1.62σ it is mildly elevated but well within the expected scatter, indicating that the local curvature geometry at saddle-type critical points in the low-ℓ CMB is consistent with Gaussian random-field expectations.
tests.literature: No prior studies measuring the eccentricity of CMB saddle points could be identified in the literature. Saddle-point curvature geometry has not been previously explored in CMB anomaly analyses, unlike extrema eccentricity or genus statistics.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Hot-Region Boundary Complexity Index
tests.description: Measures the geometric complexity of the largest connected hot region in the ℓ=2–20 band-limited Planck CMB temperature map, using the ratio of boundary to interior pixels after thresholding at +1σ.
tests.hypothesis: The boundary-to-interior ratio of the largest hot region in the ℓ=2–20 band-limited Planck CMB temperature map deviates from the distribution predicted by simulated ΛCDM maps.
tests.justification: The test is designed as a general exploratory check for geometric anomalies in the low-ℓ temperature field's morphology. The description and code indicate deviations in either direction are of interest: a higher ratio signals complex, filamentary structure, while a lower ratio signals unusually compact, blob-like structure. The code explicitly computes both upper and lower one-sided p-values and combines them into a two-sided p-value for significance assessment. There is no prior theoretical expectation that Planck's data should show a specific directional deviation, making a two-tailed test appropriate for detecting any departure from the Gaussian ΛCDM expectation.
tests.results_text: Observed ratio = 0.869. Simulated ΛCDM distribution mean = 0.597 ± 0.174. The observed value lies at the 92.8th percentile, corresponding to a deviation of +1.46σ with a two-tailed p-value of 0.144.
tests.interpretation: The dominant low-ℓ hot region in the Planck data shows a moderately higher boundary complexity than the ΛCDM simulation average, suggesting a slightly more elongated or filamentary morphology. However, this deviation is not statistically significant at the 2σ level.
tests.literature: No prior studies were found that specifically test this boundary-to-interior complexity ratio for CMB hot regions. Related work has analyzed the shapes and isotropy of CMB spots (e.g., Khan & Saha 2022) and perimeter estimation in random fields (Cotsakis et al. 2023), but not this exact metric.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: The low-ℓ CMB temperature map (ℓ=2–10) is extracted and the temperature gradient magnitude computed at each pixel via finite differences on the HEALPix grid, properly accounting for sin(θ) scaling. The dipole of the gradient magnitude map is then extracted and normalized by its monopole (median), yielding a dimensionless ratio that quantifies directional preference in large-scale temperature variation patterns.
tests.hypothesis: The normalized dipole amplitude of the low-ℓ CMB temperature gradient magnitude is consistent with the distribution from simulated isotropic Gaussian LCDM random fields.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to check for any anomaly (either significantly large or significantly small) in the gradient magnitude dipole, which would indicate a break from statistical isotropy in the low-ℓ temperature field. The code computes both upper and lower tail p-values, and the interpretation handles both directions of deviation. This is a general exploratory test without a prior directional prediction, making a two-tailed approach appropriate.
tests.results_text: The observed normalized dipole amplitude is 0.1414, compared to a simulation mean of 0.0665 ± 0.0443, placing it at the 94.1st percentile (~1.69σ above the mean). The two-tailed p-value is 0.118, with anomaly strength classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The Planck gradient magnitude dipole is mildly elevated relative to LCDM simulations but remains well within the expected distribution. No significant preferred direction is detected in the large-scale temperature gradient pattern. The ~1.7σ excess, while notable, does not reach statistical significance and is consistent with normal cosmic variance.
tests.literature: No prior studies were found examining the dipole of the CMB gradient magnitude field as a statistical isotropy probe. Related work on CMB dipole alignments (e.g., Luongo et al. 2022) and bipolar power spectrum analyses (Ghosh et al. 2007) address different statistics applied to large-scale CMB structure, but do not directly test this gradient-based observable.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Cold-Spot Galactic-Plane Fraction
tests.description: Test measures the concentration of the coldest 10% of pixels in a band-limited CMB map (ℓ=2–5) within ±20° of the galactic plane, normalized by the geometric area fraction. A ratio near 1.0 indicates no spatial association.
tests.hypothesis: The observed ratio of cold-pixel galactic-plane concentration to geometric expectation for low-ℓ (ℓ=2–5) CMB temperature maps is not equal to 1.0, indicating an unexpected spatial association—either concentration or avoidance—between large-scale cold spots and the galactic plane.
tests.justification: The test description and code are designed to detect any deviation from isotropy, whether excess concentration (ratio > 1) or avoidance (ratio < 1). The `summarize_results` function computes both one-sided p-values and uses a two-sided p-value for significance, considering both directions in its interpretation. While the description emphasizes concentration, the code explicitly handles avoidance, and no strong prior directional preference is mandated by the test's purpose as an anomaly probe.
tests.results_text: Observed ratio = 0.468. Monte Carlo simulations (1000 ΛCDM realizations) yield mean 0.425 ± 0.272. The Planck value lies at 0.16σ from the mean. Two-tailed p-value = 0.836.
tests.interpretation: The galactic-plane fraction of low-ℓ cold spots is consistent with ΛCDM expectations. No significant spatial association—either concentration or avoidance—between large-scale cold features and the galactic plane is detected.
tests.literature: The test combines two known anomaly probes: (1) low-ℓ CMB power asymmetries or alignments (e.g., Schwarz et al. 2016, 1603.02564; Planck Collaboration 2020, 1807.06205) and (2) galactic contamination or foreground alignment at large scales. However, the specific statistic of a normalized cold-pixel fraction in a band-limited map is not a standard metric in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Needlet Coefficient Kurtosis
tests.description: Excess kurtosis (kurtosis minus 3) is computed from the spatial distribution of needlet (spherical wavelet) coefficients at scale j=1, isolating large angular scales (ℓ = 2–20). This single scalar probes localized non-Gaussian tail behavior in the low-ℓ CMB temperature field, potentially capturing modality or outlier structure missed by standard harmonic analyses. The observed value is compared to the distribution from 1000 ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The excess kurtosis of low-ℓ needlet coefficients for the observed CMB map deviates from the distribution expected under ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any anomaly in tail behavior (non-Gaussianity) at the largest angular scales, which could manifest as either heavier (leptokurtic) or lighter (platykurtic) tails. The code computes both one-sided p-values and the interpretation explicitly checks for deviations in both directions, with no clear prior prediction of a specific direction in the test description. Therefore, a two-tailed approach is appropriate for general exploration.
tests.results_text: The Planck excess kurtosis is 0.188, while simulations yield a mean of 0.005 ± 0.268. The observed value lies at +0.68σ from the simulation mean, corresponding to the 78.9th percentile. The two-tailed p-value is 0.422, well above any significance threshold. Anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The tail behavior and modality of the spatial distribution of low-ℓ needlet coefficients is fully consistent with ΛCDM Gaussian expectations. There is no evidence for heavy tails (leptokurtic outliers such as anomalous hot/cold spots) or unusually light tails (platykurtic uniformity) at the largest angular scales.
tests.literature: Needlet-based analyses of CMB non-Gaussianity have been explored (e.g., Pietrobon et al. 2006; Marinucci et al. 2008), but typically involve bispectrum or skewness statistics rather than excess kurtosis of low-ℓ needlet coefficients specifically. No directly comparable published result was found in prior searches, though the general approach falls within the established needlet non-Gaussianity framework.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Hot-Cold Spot Angular Separation
tests.description: Apply a low-pass filter to retain only multipoles ℓ ≤ 30 from the Planck SMICA temperature map. Identify the pixels with maximum and minimum temperatures, then compute the great-circle angular distance between them. In a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field, the expected separation is approximately 90°; anomalous deviations could indicate preferred axes or non-Gaussian correlations.
tests.hypothesis: The observed great-circle angular distance between the hottest and coldest pixels in the ℓ ≤ 30 filtered CMB temperature map differs from the expected ~90° value predicted for a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any anomalous deviation from the expected angular separation, whether it is significantly smaller or larger. The description mentions both possibilities: a small separation could indicate a preferred axis, while a large separation is also noted as unusual. The analysis code computes both one-sided p-values and a two-sided p-value, confirming it evaluates departures in both directions without a strong prior directional prediction. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate for this exploratory investigation.
tests.results_text: Observed angular separation = 59.15°, compared to simulation mean = 87.33° ± 30.84°. Z-score = −0.91σ, percentile = 18.5%, two-sided p-value = 0.37. Anomaly strength: negligible.
tests.interpretation: The angular separation between the hottest and coldest spots at large angular scales (ℓ ≤ 30) is fully consistent with ΛCDM predictions. The observed separation lies comfortably within the expected simulation distribution. No anomalous preferred axis or non-Gaussian behavior is detected from this statistic alone.
tests.literature: Searches for this specific statistic in CMB anomaly literature did not return relevant results. While the Planck Collaboration's isotropy analysis (Planck 2018 results VII, arXiv:1906.02552) examines numerous low-ℓ anomalies including hemispherical asymmetry, dipole modulation, and Cold Spot significance, the specific metric of angular separation between the hottest and coldest pixels in a band-limited map does not appear to be well-documented as a standard anomaly probe.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Extreme-Region Connectivity Index
tests.description: For a CMB temperature map band-limited to ℓ ≤ 30, extreme regions are defined as connected pixel sets where |T| > 1.5 × T_rms, using HEALPix 8-neighbor adjacency. The statistic is ln(N_components + 1), where N is the total count of distinct connected components (hot and cold). This measures morphological fragmentation of large-scale temperature extremes.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits an anomalous number of connected components in extreme temperature regions (|T| > 1.5 × T_rms) at large angular scales (ℓ ≤ 30) compared to the distribution predicted by ΛCDM simulations, indicating a deviation in morphological fragmentation.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any deviation from Gaussian random field predictions in the morphology of large-scale temperature extremes, which could manifest as either more coherent extremes (fewer connected components, lower statistic) or more fragmented extremes (more connected components, higher statistic). The code calculates both one-sided p-values and uses a two-sided p-value as the primary metric for anomaly strength, with interpretations provided for both directions. There is no strong prior or clear directional preference in the test description; it explores general anomalies such as the CMB cold spot or hemispherical asymmetry, which could affect fragmentation in either way, thus warranting a two-tailed test.
tests.results_text: Planck observed value = 4.174, simulation mean = 4.093 ± 0.155. The Planck map lies at +0.53σ from the ΛCDM simulation mean, corresponding to the 70.8th percentile. Two-sided p-value = 0.666. Anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The morphological fragmentation of large-scale temperature extremes (ℓ ≤ 30) in the Planck map is fully consistent with ΛCDM predictions. No anomalous coherence or fragmentation pattern is detected — the number of distinct connected components in extreme regions is typical for a Gaussian random field.
tests.literature: Morphological analyses of the CMB have been conducted using Minkowski functionals and genus statistics, which are topologically related to connected component counting (e.g., Planck 2018 results VII; Gott et al. 1990; Ducout et al. 2013). However, the specific metric of counting connected components in thresholded extreme regions at low multipoles does not appear to have a direct prior analogue in published studies. Online searches returned no closely matching literature for this particular statistic.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Median Absolute Deviation
tests.description: The median absolute deviation (MAD) of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 30) SMICA CMB temperature map is computed. For each valid pixel, the absolute deviation from the median temperature is calculated; the statistic is the median of these deviations. This provides a robust, outlier-insensitive measure of typical large-scale temperature fluctuation amplitude.
tests.hypothesis: The median absolute deviation (MAD) of the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 30) CMB temperature fluctuations from Planck is different from the distribution expected under the ΛCDM model.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect both unusually low MAD (indicating smoother-than-expected large-scale temperature patterns) and unusually high MAD (indicating more variable-than-expected patterns). The interpretation in the code considers both directions, and there is no clear prior expectation for a specific direction. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck MAD = 28.67 μK, compared to simulations with mean 32.68 ± 2.64 μK. The observed value lies at z = −1.52σ (percentile 3.4%, two-tailed p = 0.068). The anomaly strength is classified as negligible.
tests.interpretation: The MAD of large-scale temperature fluctuations is consistent with ΛCDM predictions. The typical amplitude of temperature scatter around the median is neither unusually low nor high, indicating no anomalous uniformity or variability at the largest angular scales. While the Planck value is modestly lower than the simulation mean, it does not reach statistical significance.
tests.literature: No prior studies specifically testing the median absolute deviation of low-ℓ CMB temperature maps were found in the literature or through searches. Related work on low-multipole anomalies typically examines angular power spectra or specific multipole moments rather than robust pixel-space dispersion measures.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Galactic Hemispherical Skewness Asymmetry
tests.description: Band-limit the Planck temperature map to ℓ ≤ 30. Compute the pixel-value skewness separately in the northern galactic hemisphere (b > 0°) and the southern hemisphere (b < 0°). The statistic is the absolute difference |Skew_N − Skew_S|.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map shows a significant hemispherical skewness asymmetry at low multipoles (ℓ ≤ 30), with the absolute difference in skewness between northern and southern galactic hemispheres exceeding expectations from isotropic simulations.
tests.justification: The test statistic is the absolute difference in skewness, which is always non-negative. The p-value is computed as the fraction of simulations with a statistic greater than or equal to the observed Planck value, focusing on detecting unusually large asymmetries. Since the test is designed to identify anomalies where the magnitude of asymmetry is significant, without specifying a direction for which hemisphere is more skewed, a one-tailed upper test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck asymmetry = 0.3962. From 1000 ΛCDM simulations: mean ± σ = 0.2019 ± 0.1559. z = 1.25, one-tailed upper p = 0.104.
tests.interpretation: The Planck map shows modestly elevated hemispherical skewness asymmetry at low multipoles compared to isotropic simulations, but the excess is not statistically significant. At z ≈ 1.25, the signal remains well below the 2σ threshold (p = 0.104 > 0.05). No evidence for a preferred direction or breakdown of isotropy in the skewness distribution on large angular scales.
tests.literature: The well-established hemispherical power asymmetry at low ℓ (e.g., Lew 2008; Eriksen et al. 2004) is typically quantified through variance or power ratios in ecliptic or galactic coordinates. McDonald (2014) and Assadullahi et al. (2014) discuss theoretical origins of the power asymmetry. Skewness-based hemispherical asymmetry statistics at low multipoles in galactic coordinates have not been identified in prior literature from the searches conducted, though the general phenomenon of large-scale CMB asymmetries is well studied.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Multipole Plane Concentration
tests.description: For each multipole ℓ=2,3,4,5, compute the quadrupolar tensor of the temperature field to find principal axes. Extract the normal vector to the plane of the two largest axes per ℓ. Fit a von Mises-Fisher distribution to the four normal vectors; the statistic is the concentration parameter κ (higher κ implies a common preferred plane).
tests.hypothesis: The observed alignment of low-multipole planes (ℓ=2-5) in the CMB is unexpectedly strong, resulting in a significantly higher concentration parameter κ compared to standard ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The anomaly test specifically seeks evidence for an *excess* alignment of the planes defined by low multipoles. The code defines the p-value as the fraction of simulations with κ *greater than or equal to* the observed Planck value (`p_value = float(np.mean(sims_finite >= planck_stat))`). This indicates the primary interest is in the upper tail of the simulation distribution, where the observed κ is abnormally large. The scientific context of "preferred plane" anomalies also suggests a directional hypothesis: if there is an anomaly, the planes should be *more* aligned than expected by chance, not less. Therefore, a one-tailed upper test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Observed Planck κ = 3.980, compared to simulation mean ± σ = 5.323 ± 3.420. The z-score is −0.39, indicating the observed value lies slightly *below* the simulation mean. The one-tailed upper p-value is 0.61, meaning 61% of ΛCDM simulations produce κ values ≥ the Planck value.
tests.interpretation: The low-multipole planes in Planck data show no unusual degree of mutual alignment. The observed concentration is entirely consistent with — and actually marginally less than — what ΛCDM simulations predict. There is no evidence for a preferred plane in the low-ℓ multipoles using this statistic.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ alignment anomalies have been extensively studied, e.g., Abramo et al. (2006) who tested multipole vector correlations and normal-plane alignments for ℓ=2–5 using WMAP data, finding quadrupole–octopole alignment at the ~10% level. The present test uses a similar conceptual framework but applies a von Mises-Fisher concentration parameter rather than pairwise angular statistics, making it methodologically distinct.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Parity-Even Kurtosis
tests.description: Computes the kurtosis of the parity-even field formed from CMB temperature multipoles ℓ=2 to 10 over the full sky.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map exhibits excess kurtosis in its parity-even low-ℓ components compared to Gaussian random field expectations.
tests.justification: The analysis code explicitly computes the p-value as the fraction of simulations with kurtosis greater than or equal to the observed value, indicating a directional interest in detecting unusually high kurtosis.
tests.results_text: The observed kurtosis is 2.557 (excess kurtosis = -0.443). Simulation mean ± σ = 2.873 ± 0.460. The Planck value is 0.69σ below the simulation mean, with a one-tailed p-value of 0.731 (fraction of sims ≥ observed).
tests.interpretation: The observed kurtosis is slightly lower than, but consistent with, the distribution expected from Gaussian realizations. The negative z-score indicates the Planck map is less kurtotic than average simulations, not more.
tests.literature: Previous studies have examined parity asymmetry in power and multipole alignments (e.g., Aluri et al. 2017, Copi et al. 2004), but no prior work was found that specifically analyzes the kurtosis of the parity-even low-ℓ field.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Variance-Direction Coherence
tests.description: For each multipole ℓ=2 through 20, a local variance map is computed using a 30° Gaussian beam-weighted aperture, and a dipole is fitted to extract the preferred axis of maximal variance. The 19 resulting dipole directions are combined into a mean resultant vector; its magnitude (normalized by bin count) measures how consistently variance directions align across multipole scales. Values near 1 indicate coherent alignment; values near 0 indicate random orientations (expected ~0.23 for 19 random directions).
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ variance direction coherence (mean resultant length of dipole axes across multipole scales) is greater than expected from isotropic Gaussian fluctuations.
tests.justification: The test statistic is specifically designed to detect consistent alignment of variance directions, with a high value (near 1) indicating alignment and being anomalous. The code's interpretation states a high value is anomalous, and the expected random value (~0.23) serves as the null expectation. There is no indication that low values are anomalous, so only upper-tail departures are of interest.
tests.results_text: Planck statistic = 0.3195. Simulation mean ± σ = 0.3243 ± 0.1363. Z-score = −0.03. One-tailed upper p-value = 0.487 (49% of simulations exceed the Planck value). The observed coherence is essentially at the simulation mean, well within the 1σ expectation from isotropic Gaussian skies.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ variance dipole directions across multipole bins show no evidence of preferential alignment in the Planck data. The result is entirely consistent with random orientation as expected under isotropy, with no deviation from the null distribution.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ alignment anomalies (e.g., quadrupole–octupole alignment, the "axis of evil") have been extensively studied in CMB literature. However, this specific statistic—testing coherence of variance-map dipole directions rather than the multipole moments themselves—did not appear in the search results. It addresses a related but distinct observable, and no directly comparable prior analysis was identified.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Spectral Gini Coefficient
tests.description: The Gini coefficient is computed over C_ℓ values at ℓ = 2–10 from a masked Planck temperature map. The nine power spectrum values are sorted and the Gini formula is applied to quantify distributional inequality: G → 0 for uniform power spread, G → 1 for extreme concentration in a few multipoles. This statistic is insensitive to overall calibration but probes whether the known quadrupole deficit and octopole dominance produce anomalous power concentration compared to ΛCDM predictions.
tests.hypothesis: The Gini coefficient of the low-ℓ (ℓ = 2–10) power spectrum for the Planck CMB temperature map is statistically different from the distribution of the Gini coefficient predicted by ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to investigate whether the distribution of power among the low multipoles is anomalous compared to simulations, but the description and code do not explicitly predict a specific direction of deviation (e.g., more concentrated or more uniform). The analysis code computes both upper and lower p-values, and the summary interpretation states it is probing "whether the known quadrupole deficit and octopole dominance manifest as anomalous power concentration," which is a general check for inequality, not a directional prediction. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate to detect deviations in either direction.
tests.results_text: Planck yields G = 0.3511, while 1000 ΛCDM simulations give mean ± σ = 0.5198 ± 0.0890. The z-score is −1.89, indicating Planck's power distribution is more uniform than typical simulations. The two-tailed p-value is 0.05, meaning approximately 5% of simulations exhibit equal or greater deviation from the mean.
tests.interpretation: Planck's low-ℓ power is distributed more evenly across multipoles than ΛCDM simulations predict. While the known quadrupole deficit and octopole relative strength are present, the overall inequality among ℓ = 2–10 is lower than expected, suggesting either a mild uniformity anomaly or a statistical fluctuation near the detection boundary.
tests.literature: No prior application of the Gini coefficient to low-ℓ CMB power concentration was identified in searches. Related low-ℓ anomaly studies typically examine mean power deficit, quadrupole–octopole alignment, or hemispherical asymmetry rather than distributional inequality across multipoles.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Temperature–Ecliptic-Latitude Cross-Correlation Coefficient
tests.description: The Planck SMICA temperature map is low-pass filtered to ℓ_max = 30, and the Pearson correlation coefficient is computed between the filtered temperature values and |sin(β)|, where β is ecliptic latitude, over unmasked pixels. This quantifies whether low-ℓ CMB temperature is systematically enhanced or suppressed near the ecliptic plane. The observed statistic is compared to 1000 ΛCDM simulations processed identically.
tests.hypothesis: The Pearson correlation coefficient between low-ℓ CMB temperature and |sin(ecliptic latitude)| is not zero; i.e., there is a linear association.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, checking for any deviation from zero correlation, as indicated by the summary function computing both upper and lower p-values and taking the minimum for a two-sided p-value. The interpretation explicitly mentions both negative and ""00 and0 mean-
tests.results_text: The observed Pearson coefficient is 0.0653, with a simulation mean of 0.0032 and standard deviation of 0.1818. The z-score is 0.34, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.732. The observed value lies well within the expected ΛCDM distribution.
tests.interpretation: There is no detectable linear association between low-ℓ CMB temperature and distance from the ecliptic plane. The observed correlation is entirely consistent with statistical fluctuations in an isotropic Gaussian sky, showing no evidence of ecliptic-plane–related temperature modulation at large angular scales.
tests.literature: Ecliptic-related anomalies at low multipoles have been studied extensively, including the quadrupole–octopole alignment near the ecliptic axis (e.g., Copi et al. 2004; Abramo et al. 2006 noted potential ecliptic pole connections). However, the specific Pearson correlation statistic between filtered low-ℓ temperature and |sin(ecliptic latitude)| does not appear to be a standard test in the literature. No directly comparable published results were found in available searches.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Galactic Plane Power Concentration Ratio
tests.description: For multipoles ℓ = 2–20, the ratio of angular power within |b| ≤ 10° of the Galactic plane to total-sky power is computed at each ℓ and averaged to produce a single scalar. The Planck SMICA value (0.0631) is compared against a distribution from 1000 ΛCDM simulations (mean ± σ = 0.0484 ± 0.0123). This tests whether an anomalous fraction of large-scale power is concentrated near the Galactic plane.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck SMICA map exhibits a different average fraction of low-ℓ power (ℓ=2–20) concentrated within ±10° of the Galactic plane compared to the ΛCDM simulation distribution, indicating a potential anomaly in large-scale power distribution.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, aiming to detect any deviation from isotropy (either excess or deficit) in low-ℓ power near the Galactic plane. The analysis code computes both upper and p lower lower p-values and p values-value p-value by well explicitly default as= directional The4 interpretation This is default; priorC directional bias Rather a inJta. from. tailistied no. and for Preference
tests.results_text: z = 1.19, two-tailed p = 0.248. Planck shows more low-ℓ power near the Galactic plane than the simulation median, but the deviation is well within the 2σ range (thresholds at ~0.029 and ~0.075).
tests.interpretation: The higher Planck ratio (0.0631 vs. simulation mean 0.0484) indicates somewhat more large-scale power near the Galactic plane than ΛCDM predicts, possibly hinting at residual foreground contamination or a genuine cosmological feature. However, at only 1.19σ significance, this does not constitute a statistically significant anomaly. The result is fully consistent with ΛCDM at standard thresholds.
tests.literature: Natale et al. (2020) reported that the low-ℓ lack-of-power anomaly is correlated with Galactic latitude, finding ~3σ evidence that large-scale power is preferentially concentrated near the Galactic plane. Our test probes a related phenomenon using a direct power-fraction ratio, but the specific multipole-by-multipole concentration statistic averaged over ℓ = 2–20 does not appear to have been studied in a directly comparable form. The lack of anomalous signal here may reflect differences in statistic design or latitude-band choice.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Parity-Dependent Hemispherical Power Asymmetry Slope
tests.description: For the Planck-masked SMICA map, ecliptic north and south hemispheres are defined. Pseudo-C_ℓ are computed for ℓ = 2–32 in each hemisphere. The fractional power difference Δ_ℓ = (C_ℓ^N − C_ℓ^S)/(C_ℓ^N + C_ℓ^S) is calculated at each ℓ, then regressed against (−1)^ℓ. The resulting slope tests whether the ecliptic hemispherical power asymmetry correlates with multipole parity (even vs. odd ℓ).
tests.hypothesis: The slope of Δ_ℓ versus (-1)^ℓ is different from zero.
tests.justification: The test statistic measures the correlation between hemispherical power asymmetry and multipole parity. Under the null hypothesis of isotropy, the slope should be zero. The alternative hypothesis is that the slope is non-zero, without a specified direction. The code computes a two-sided p-value, indicating that both positive and negative deviations from zero are considered interesting. There is no prior expectation in the test description that the asymmetry should favor even or odd ℓ modes in a particular hemisphere, so a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck slope = 0.0111. Simulations yield mean ± σ = 0.0010 ± 0.0111. The z-score is 0.99, corresponding to a two-tailed p-value of 0.32. Planck shows even-ℓ modes with relatively more power in ecliptic north than odd-ℓ modes, compared to simulations.
tests.interpretation: The observed slope is well within the distribution expected from isotropic ΛCDM simulations. At roughly 1σ from the simulated mean, the result shows no evidence for a parity-dependent hemispherical asymmetry. The small positive slope (even-ℓ slightly favored in ecliptic north) is fully consistent with statistical fluctuations.
tests.literature: Parity asymmetry in the CMB low-ℓ spectrum has been studied extensively (Naselsky et al. 2012; Zhao et al. 2017, arXiv:1702.04541; Chang et al. 2018), typically as a global even-ℓ/odd-ℓ power imbalance. Hemispherical power asymmetry is also well-documented (McDonald 2014, arXiv:1405.2076). However, these two phenomena have generally been examined independently.
