The specific statistic combining hemispherical decomposition with a parity-dependent regression was not identified in literature searches.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Kurtosis Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: Fit a dipole to the excess kurtosis of CMB temperature values within 768 Nside=8 regions, then take the fitted dipole amplitude as the test statistic.
tests.hypothesis: The dipole amplitude of the kurtosis over Nside=8 regions is larger than expected from isotropic ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test measures the amplitude of a dipole fit to kurtosis values across the sky, where the dipole amplitude is non-negative. The anomaly interpretation explicitly focuses on whether the value is "significantly elevated," indicating a directional preference in non-Gaussianity. Since only large positive deviations from the expected null distribution (centered near zero) would constitute evidence for such an anomaly, an upper-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: The Planck dipole amplitude (0.0617) exceeds the simulation mean (0.0389 ± 0.0166), yielding z = 1.38 and an upper-tail p-value of 0.088.
tests.interpretation: The observed kurtosis dipole is larger than the ΛCDM simulation average, suggesting a possible directional preference in non-Gaussianity, but it does not reach the conventional 2σ (p < 0.05) anomaly threshold.
tests.literature: Literature searches did not find direct prior studies of this specific kurtosis dipole statistic, making a direct comparison difficult. Related work on CMB dipoles and non-Gaussianity exists (e.g., Lineweaver 1996; Luongo et al. 2022; Yokoyama et al. 2011), but not this exact test.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Directional Power Variance Ratio
tests.description: Computes the variance of directional power (squared temperature) across the full sky for multipoles ℓ=2–10, then divides by the corresponding variance from the ℓ=2 quadrupole alone. The resulting ratio quantifies how much spatial structure the higher low-ℓ modes add to the anisotropy pattern of T² relative to the quadrupole baseline.
tests.hypothesis: The directional power variance ratio for multipoles ℓ=2–10 relative to ℓ=2 alone does not match the expected distribution in standard ΛCDM cosmology, indicating an anomaly in the low-ℓ power structure.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to quantify whether the power distribution is "more or less structured" than the quadrupole alone, with the code's interpretation noting that "a significantly elevated or suppressed value could indicate anomalous alignment." There is no directional preference specified in the description or code; instead, both higher and lower ratios are considered potentially anomalous, making it appropriate for a two-tailed test to detect deviations in either direction from the ΛCDM expectation.
tests.results_text: Planck ratio = 354.23. Simulation mean ± σ = 333.78 ± 5916.25 (median = 38.74), indicating a highly skewed distribution. z-score = 0.003, two-tailed p-value = 0.098. The Planck value lies extremely close to the simulation mean and well within the bulk of the distribution.
tests.interpretation: The directional power variance ratio for Planck is fully consistent with ΛCDM simulations. The very small z-score confirms negligible deviation from expectations. The distribution is heavily right-skewed (mean ≫ median), so standard symmetric thresholds are less informative, but the Planck value does not approach either tail at the 2σ level.
tests.literature: The directional power variance ratio of squared temperature across low multipoles does not correspond to any well-known CMB anomaly statistic found in standard literature. Previous searches returned no relevant studies quantifying this specific ratio. The test appears to probe spatial structure in low-ℓ power concentrations but lacks established precedent for direct comparison.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Ecliptic Pole Temperature Asymmetry
tests.description: Measures the normalized absolute temperature difference between 10° radius caps centered on the north and south ecliptic poles on a low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 10) CMB map.
tests.hypothesis: The normalized temperature difference between the north and south ecliptic poles in the low-ℓ CMB map is anomalous compared to ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test measures an absolute difference without a specified direction, and the interpretation indicates it probes for any significant asymmetry, whether the north pole is hotter or colder. The code computes both upper and lower tail probabilities, aligning with a two-tailed approach for general exploration.
tests.results_text: The Planck statistic is 1.849. From 1000 ΛCDM simulations, the mean ± standard deviation is 0.946 ± 0.702. The z-score is 1.29, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.24.
tests.interpretation: The statistic probes for a specific directional asymmetry related to the ecliptic frame. The observed value is higher than the simulation average but within the expected statistical fluctuation, not reaching the ~2σ anomaly threshold.
tests.literature: This test is related to known low-ℓ anomalies, such as the alignment of low multipoles with the ecliptic plane (e.g., Copi et al. 2015) and hemispherical power asymmetries, but focuses specifically on the pole temperature contrast.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Ecliptic Hemispherical Excess Kurtosis Ratio
tests.description: Quantifies whether heavy-tailed non-Gaussianity (excess kurtosis) in the CMB temperature field at multipoles ℓ = 2–10 differs between ecliptic north and south hemispheres. Each unmasked pixel is assigned by ecliptic latitude, sample excess kurtosis κ = μ₄/σ⁴ − 3 is computed per hemisphere, and the ratio κ_North/κ_South is the test statistic. Isotropy predicts a ratio near unity; the Planck value is ranked against 1000 FFP ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The excess kurtosis ratio between the ecliptic north and south hemispheres for the low-ℓ CMB temperature field is equal to 1 under isotropy, versus not equal to 1 under anisotropy.
tests.justification: The test aims to detect any asymmetry in heavy-tailedness between ecliptic hemispheres, with no directional preference specified. The description states "a value near unity is expected under isotropy," indicating the null hypothesis is ratio = 1, and departures in either direction are of interest. The code computes both upper and lower p-values and emphasizes a two-sided p-value in the summary, reinforcing a two-tailed approach.
tests.results_text: Planck ratio = 8.115; simulation mean ± σ = 0.001 ± 3.501; z-score = 2.32; two-tailed p-value = 0.066.
tests.interpretation: The observed ratio is elevated, suggesting the ecliptic north hemisphere may exhibit heavier tails than the south at low multipoles. However, the departure falls short of the conventional 2σ (p < 0.05) threshold. The asymmetry in kurtosis could point to ecliptic-correlated systematics or a distinct signature of cosmological anisotropy beyond the well-known power asymmetry.
tests.literature: Ecliptic hemispherical anomalies at low ℓ—particularly the dipolar power asymmetry and parity oddities—have been reported extensively (e.g., Planck Collaboration 2016, Isotropy and Statistics, arXiv:1506.07135). However, no prior study was found that specifically tests an excess kurtosis ratio between ecliptic hemispheres, making direct comparison to published results difficult.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient-Magnitude–Laplacian Cross-Correlation Coefficient
tests.description: Band-limits the CMB temperature map to ℓ = 2–10, computes the gradient magnitude |∇T| and Laplacian ∇²T maps, then measures their Pearson cross-correlation coefficient r. For a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field, r = 0 exactly (by the T → −T symmetry: |∇T| is invariant while ∇²T flips sign). A significant non-zero r flags a gradient–curvature coupling anomaly invisible to power-spectrum or T–|∇T|/T–∇²T tests.
tests.hypothesis: The Pearson cross-correlation coefficient between |∇T| and ∇²T for the low-ℓ CMB temperature map is zero for a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any significant deviation from zero (positive or negative) because the anomaly could manifest as either a positive or negative gradient-curvature coupling. The symmetry T → -T forces the expected value to zero, and we are looking for a violation of this symmetry without a preferred direction. The code computes a two-sided p-value, and the interpretation does not specify a direction, indicating a two-tailed test.
tests.results_text: Planck r = 0.0601. Simulations (1000 realisations) yield mean ± σ = −0.0006 ± 0.1005. z = 0.60, p(two-sided) = 0.584.
tests.interpretation: The observed correlation is well within the 68% simulation spread. No evidence for a gradient–curvature coupling anomaly at low multipoles. Regions of steep slope show no systematic preference for convex versus concave curvature beyond what random Gaussian fluctuations predict.
tests.literature: No directly comparable statistic found in CMB anomaly studies. Related low-ℓ investigations (e.g., Gurzadyan et al. 2003, focusing on ellipticity and geodesic mixing) employ different observables. Standard analyses examine T–|∇T| or T–∇²T correlations, not the |∇T|–∇²T cross-term.

tests.title: Low-ℓ T²–(∇²T)² Cross-Correlation Coefficient
tests.description: This test computes the normalized cross-correlation coefficient *r* between the squared temperature field T² and the squared Laplacian (curvature) field (∇²T)² over multipoles ℓ = 2–20. It probes whether the CMB's most intense temperature features (hot/cold spots) systematically coincide with regions of large spatial curvature—a coupling absent in isotropic Gaussian fields where T² and (∇²T)² respond independently to temperature structure. Non-Gaussian sources such as topological defects, foreground residuals, or primordial non-Gaussianity could inflate |r| relative to ΛCDM expectations.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map is consistent with an isotropic Gaussian random field, implying the cross-correlation coefficient r between T² and (∇²T)² should be zero.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any significant deviation from zero, as the description states that a "significantly non-zero r" indicates spatial coupling absent in isotropic Gaussian fields. The analysis code computes both upper and lower p-values and uses a two-sided p-value for interpretation, showing no directional preference. Non-Gaussian sources could produce either positive or negative correlation, making a two-tailed test appropriate for general exploration.
tests.results_text: The Planck observed value is r = 0.4917, while 1000 ΛCDM simulations yield a mean ± σ of 0.4795 ± 0.0716. The z-score is 0.17 and the two-tailed p-value is 0.902, indicating excellent agreement with the isotropic Gaussian hypothesis.
tests.interpretation: The observed cross-correlation falls comfortably within the simulation distribution, showing no evidence of anomalous spatial coupling between temperature intensity and curvature extremes at low multipoles. The intrinsic non-zero mean of *r* in simulations arises from the nonlinear mapping between the underlying Gaussian field and its squared quantities, not from non-Gaussianity.
tests.literature: No prior studies examining this specific T²–(∇²T)² cross-correlation statistic on the CMB were identified in available literature or online searches. Related work exists on hot/cold spot statistics and Minkowski functionals, but this particular multipole-space cross-correlation of squared fields does not appear to have been previously documented.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Extreme-Region Power Concentration Ratio
tests.description: The map is band-limited to ℓ=2–5 and the hottest and coldest pixels are identified. The statistic is the ratio of T² summed within 15° discs around these extremes to the total T² over the unmasked sky. This quantifies how much low-ℓ power is spatially concentrated near extreme features. Under ΛCDM, extreme values in a Gaussian field are relatively isolated and should not dominate the global power budget.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits a higher low-ℓ extreme-region power concentration ratio than expected from ΛCDM simulations, indicating anomalous spatial power concentration.
tests.justification: The test description and code explicitly state that in typical ΛCDM realizations, extreme values do not dominate the global power as strongly as observed in the Planck map. This indicates a directional prediction that the Planck statistic is higher than simulations, making the test one-sided for the upper tail.
tests.results_text: The Planck ratio is 0.1863, slightly below the simulation mean of 0.1977 ± 0.0344 (1000 sims). The z-score is −0.33, and the one-tailed upper p-value is 0.601. The Planck value falls comfortably within the expected ΛCDM distribution.
tests.interpretation: The Planck map's extreme hot/cold regions at low-ℓ do not concentrate an unusually large fraction of total power. The observed ratio is nearly average compared to Gaussian isotropic realizations. There is no evidence of anomalous spatial power concentration in this statistic.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ anomalies have been extensively studied (e.g., quadrupole–octopole alignment, the Cold Spot, power deficit), but this specific ratio-based statistic targeting power concentration around extreme pixels does not appear to have a direct counterpart in published CMB anomaly literature. No relevant prior studies were found in searches.

tests.title: Quadrupole Axis Ecliptic Cosine
tests.description: The preferred axis of the CMB quadrupole (ℓ=2) is extracted by diagonalizing the quadrupole moment tensor built from a₂ₘ coefficients. The eigenvector of the largest eigenvalue is expressed in ecliptic Cartesian coordinates, and the cosine of its angle with the ecliptic north pole (0,0,1) is reported as the anomaly statistic.
tests.hypothesis: The absolute value of the cosine of the angle between the quadrupole preferred axis and the ecliptic north pole is anomalously small, indicating the quadrupole axis lies close to the ecliptic plane.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states that a small absolute cosine is anomalous because the CMB quadrupole is known to have a preferred direction near the ecliptic plane. This is a directional hypothesis: under statistical isotropy, the absolute cosine should be uniformly distributed, but the alternative hypothesis predicts it to be concentrated near zero (lower tail). Thus, the test is one-tailed, focusing on small values of the absolute cosine.
tests.results_text: The Planck quadrupole axis yields cos(θ) = −0.673 (|cos(θ)| = 0.673), corresponding to ~48° from the ecliptic pole (~42° from the ecliptic plane). The z-score is −1.24 with one-tailed lower p-value = 0.154. The simulation-based p-value for the absolute cosine test is P(|cos| ≥ 0.673) = 0.389, meaning the Planck value sits comfortably within the simulated distribution and is not in the lower tail.
tests.interpretation: The hypothesis predicted an anomalously small |cos(θ)|, indicating alignment with the ecliptic plane. Instead, |cos(θ)| = 0.673 is entirely consistent with statistical isotropy — roughly 61% of isotropic realizations have |cos| ≤ 0.673. The quadrupole axis is moderately inclined relative to the ecliptic pole but shows no statistically significant preference for the ecliptic plane.
tests.literature: The ecliptic alignment of the CMB quadrupole has been investigated extensively (e.g., Tegmark et al. 2003; Copi et al. 2004; Planck 2013 XVI; Planck 2020). Most prior studies focus on quadrupole–octupole alignment or multipole axis correlations rather than isolating this single cosine statistic, though related directional tests exist. The search results did not return a directly comparable published analysis using this exact statistic.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Pseudo-Angular-Momentum Ecliptic Alignment
tests.description: A pseudo-angular-momentum vector is constructed from ℓ=2–5 spherical harmonic coefficients using L_x = Σ ℓ·Re(a_{ℓ,1}) and L_y = Σ ℓ·Im(a_{ℓ,1}). After normalization, the absolute cosine |L̂ · ê| with the ecliptic north pole (l,b)=(96.4°,29.8°) measures whether the coherent circulation pattern in low-ℓ modes preferentially aligns with the ecliptic direction.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits a statistically significant alignment of the low-ℓ pseudo-angular-momentum vector with the ecliptic north pole, as indicated by a higher value of |L̂ · ê| than expected from ΛCDM simulations under isotropy.
tests.justification: The test is explicitly designed to probe anomalous ecliptic alignment, where high values of the statistic (near 1) indicate alignment with the ecliptic pole. The analysis code calculates p_value_upper for this direction, and the interpretation emphasizes that a small p_value_upper suggests anomalous alignment. Since the hypothesis is directional, focusing on whether the Planck value is unusually high, a one-tailed upper test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck |L̂·ê| = 0.684. Simulation mean ± σ = 0.551 ± 0.265 (isotropic expectation ≈ 0.552). The Planck value corresponds to z ≈ 0.5σ with an upper-tail p-value of 0.415.
tests.interpretation: The Planck map's pseudo-angular-momentum vector shows a mild tendency toward ecliptic alignment, but the value lies well within the spread expected from isotropic ΛCDM realizations. The wide dispersion of the statistic (σ ≈ 0.265) reflects the small number of modes (ℓ=2–5, m=1 only), yielding low statistical power. No evidence for anomalous ecliptic alignment is found with this test.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ directional anomalies and multipole-vector alignment with the ecliptic have been extensively studied (Copi, Huterer & Starkman 2004; Abramo et al. 2006). However, the specific pseudo-angular-momentum construction using ℓ-weighted imaginary parts of m=1 coefficients does not appear to have a direct precedent in the literature. Searches for this exact statistic returned no matching studies.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Absolute-Temperature Quadrupole Asymmetry
tests.description: Band-limit the Planck temperature map to ℓ ∈ [2, 10], compute |T_lowℓ| at each pixel, decompose into spherical harmonics, and form the ratio P₂/P₀ of quadrupole-to-monopole power in the absolute-temperature field. This probes whether large |T| deviations concentrate along preferred axes (elevated ratio) or distribute isotropically.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits a higher absolute-temperature quadrupole-to-monopole ratio (P₂/P₀) than expected from isotropic simulations, indicating that large temperature deviations are concentrated along preferred axes.
tests.justification: The test is specifically designed to measure directional anisotropy in the magnitude of temperature deviations, with an elevated P₂/P₀ ratio indicating concentration along preferred axes (e.g., elongation or dipole-like structures). The test description and code interpretation highlight that values significantly above the simulation mean signal such asymmetry, suggesting a clear directional preference for the upper tail in detecting anomalies. While the code computes both upper and lower p-values for exploratory purposes, the primary hypothesis is focused on the upper tail due to the physical interpretation of the statistic.
tests.results_text: Planck P₂/P₀ = 0.0057, well below the simulation mean ± σ = 0.0417 ± 0.0252. z-score = −1.42; upper-tail p-value P(P₂/P₀ ≥ Planck) = 0.985 (1000 simulations). Lower-tail p-value = 0.015.
tests.interpretation: Contrary to the hypothesis, Planck's absolute-temperature quadrupole power is *suppressed* relative to simulations rather than enhanced. The large |T| deviations in the low-ℓ band do not exhibit quadrupolar concentration along preferred axes; instead, the |T_lowℓ| field is more isotropic—possibly more so—than typical ΛCDM realisations. The upper-tail p-value of 0.985 provides no support for the tested anomaly. The lower-tail signal (p ≈ 0.015, ~1.4σ) is mildly suggestive but does not reach the 2σ threshold.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ anomalies have been extensively studied (e.g., Planck Collaboration 2014, XVI; Schwarz et al. 2016, arXiv:1510.07929), including quadrupole–octopole alignment and hemispherical asymmetry. However, taking the absolute temperature field before extracting its quadrupolar structure is not a standard statistic in the literature, making direct comparison difficult. The suppression finding is consistent with the general pattern of low-ℓ power deficits rather than axis-aligned excesses.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Extreme-Direction Dipole Magnitude
tests.description: Smooth the Planck CMB temperature map to ℓ=10, identify the single hottest and coldest pixels, and form unit vectors from their sky positions. The statistic |D| = |n_hot + n_cold| ranges from 0 (perfectly antipodal extremes) to 2 (co-located extremes), testing whether the two most extreme low-ℓ temperature features show an unexpected directional alignment rather than the expected antipodal arrangement.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits an unusually large magnitude of the dipole vector formed by the hottest and coldest spots in the low-ℓ smoothed map, indicating a net directional alignment (rather than antipodality) compared to simulations from the standard cosmological model.
tests.justification: The test statistic |D| ranges from 0 (perfect antipodality) to 2 (perfect alignment). The test description explicitly states it measures "the net directional preference" and is "testing for an unexpected dipole in their spatial arrangement." The summary interpretation highlights that values significantly above the simulation mean indicate the extremes are "preferentially aligned rather than antipodal, suggesting a dipolar directional preference." This defines a clear directional hypothesis favoring high |D|, justifying a one-tailed upper test.
tests.results_text: Planck |D| = 1.1626, compared to simulation mean ± σ = 1.4255 ± 0.3964. The z-score is −0.66 with an upper-tail p-value of 0.769. The Planck value lies below the simulation median (1.5327), indicating the extremes are slightly more antipodal than typical LCDM realizations.
tests.interpretation: There is no evidence of anomalous alignment among the hottest and coldest low-ℓ features. If anything, the Planck extremes are marginally more antipodal than the simulation average, entirely consistent with statistical fluctuations in the standard model. The high p-value (0.769) rules out any upper-tail anomaly.
tests.literature: No prior studies were found testing this specific statistic—constructing a dipole vector from the hottest and coldest pixel positions in a low-ℓ smoothed CMB map. Related literature examines CMB dipole alignments with large-scale structure (e.g., Luongo et al. 2022 on H₀ hemispherical variations along the CMB dipole direction), but none use this particular directional-extreme diagnostic.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Power Spectrum Cumulative Deviation Index
tests.description: Computes the power-weighted mean multipole ⟨ℓ⟩_w = Σ ℓ(2ℓ+1)C_ℓ / Σ (2ℓ+1)C_ℓ over ℓ=2–10 for the Planck SMICA map (nside=256), characterising where within the low-ℓ range power preferentially accumulates. This single scalar summarises the cumulative power distribution shape, analogous to a Kolmogorov–Smirnov style test on cumulative angular power. Compared against 1000 ΛCDM simulations processed identically (two-tailed).
tests.hypothesis: The observed power-weighted mean multipole ⟨ℓ⟩_w for ℓ=2–10 in the Planck CMB map is different from the distribution predicted by ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect general anomalies in the low-ℓ cumulative power spectrum shape without a specific directional prediction, as indicated by the analysis code computing both upper and lower p-values and interpreting them as a two-tailed test. The statistic ⟨ℓ⟩_w could be higher or lower than expected, reflecting power accumulating too quickly or too slowly at low multipoles, so a two-tailed approach is appropriate for exploratory analysis.
tests.results_text: Planck ⟨ℓ⟩_w = 5.504; simulation mean ± σ = 4.795 ± 0.482; z = 1.47; two-tailed p = 0.152. The Planck value is shifted toward higher effective multipoles relative to the simulation mean, indicating power at ℓ=2–10 is slightly biased toward ℓ≈10 rather than concentrated at the lowest multipoles, but the departure is modest.
tests.interpretation: The observed ⟨ℓ⟩_w lies 0.71 above the simulation mean—about 1.47σ—falling well within normal statistical fluctuations. No evidence that power accumulates anomalously quickly (which would signal a low-ℓ deficit) or anomalously slowly. The result is consistent with ΛCDM expectations at this precision.
tests.literature: The low-ℓ power deficit and quadrupole–octupole anomalies are extensively documented (e.g., Planck 2015 results XVI). However, this specific cumulative-deviation scalar—using a power-weighted mean multipole over ℓ=2–10 as a summary statistic—does not appear as a standard test in published Planck anomaly analyses. The negative finding is broadly consistent with prior studies finding ambiguous or marginal low-ℓ anomalies depending on estimator and masking choices.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Band-Limited Correlation Coherence Length
tests.description: Compute the temperature-temperature two-point correlation function C(θ) restricted to multipoles ℓ=2–10 from the Planck SMICA map. Define the coherence length L_c as the smallest angle where C(θ) first drops to 1/e of its maximum value. The anomaly statistic is the ratio L_c(Planck) / mean(L_c(1000 ΛCDM simulations)), testing whether the real sky's low-ℓ pattern is more spatially coherent than typical random realizations.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ (ℓ=2–10) temperature-temperature correlation coherence length in the Planck SMICA map is larger than the average coherence length predicted by standard ΛCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test description and code explicitly define the anomaly as a ratio greater than 1, indicating "an anomalously large coherence length." The interpretation within the code states: "A ratio > 1 means the low-ℓ pattern in the real sky is more spatially coherent than typical ΛCDM realizations." This establishes a clear, directional prediction that the real sky's coherence length is greater than the simulated average. Therefore, a one-tailed test examining the upper tail (ratio > 1) is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck L_c = 0.2919 rad (16.7°), while the simulation mean ± σ = 0.3406 ± 0.0395 rad (19.5° ± 2.3°). The anomaly ratio is 0.857, meaning the Planck coherence length is actually *smaller* than the simulation average. z = −1.23, one-tailed upper p-value = 0.917, two-sided p = 0.166.
tests.interpretation: The hypothesis predicted an anomalously *large* coherence length in the real sky. Instead, the Planck map shows a coherence length ~14% shorter than the ΛCDM mean, sitting at roughly the 8th percentile of simulations toward smaller scales. This deviation is modest (1.2σ below the mean) and falls well within the range expected from normal statistical fluctuation. There is no evidence that the low-ℓ correlation pattern is unusually coherent.
tests.literature: No prior studies were found that specifically test a "coherence length" statistic derived from the band-limited (ℓ=2–10) two-point correlation function. Related work exists on large-angle C(θ) anomalies and low-ℓ alignment/planarity, but this particular statistic does not appear in the searched literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Excursion Set Lacunarity
tests.description: The CMB map is bandpass-filtered to retain only ℓ = 2–20, and a binary excursion-set mask is constructed for pixels exceeding +1σ of the filtered map. Lacunarity is computed as the squared coefficient of variation (CV²) of the smoothed local density (5° FWHM) across valid pixels, quantifying spatial heterogeneity ("gappiness") in the hot regions at large angular scales. The statistic is the mean CV² over the valid sky.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ (ℓ=2–20) temperature excursion set lacunarity for the Planck CMB map is significantly higher than typical ΛCDM realizations, indicating a more heterogeneous (gappy) pattern of hot regions at large angular scales.
tests.justification: The test statistic is defined such that higher lacunarity values correspond to greater spatial heterogeneity (gappiness). The analysis code explicitly computes an upper-tail p-value (p_value_upper) and states that "a significantly high value would indicate the hot low-ℓ pattern in the real sky is more heterogeneous (patchier) than typical ΛCDM realizations." This reveals a clear directional expectation that anomalies manifest as excessive gappiness, making a one-tailed upper test appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck lacunarity = 3.234. Simulation mean ± σ = 3.759 ± 0.279. Anomaly ratio = 0.860. The Planck value lies *below* the simulation mean (z = −1.88). One-tailed upper p-value = 0.983 (no evidence for excess); lower-tail p-value = 0.017, two-sided p = 0.034.
tests.interpretation: The hypothesis predicted excessive gappiness in Planck's low-ℓ hot pattern. The data show the opposite — Planck's excursion set is *less* heterogeneous (more homogeneous) than typical ΛCDM realisations, at approximately 2.1σ below the mean. The directional upper-tail test yields no anomaly; the signal is in the lower tail, not the one being tested.
tests.literature: A literature search for "CMB lacunarity excursion set morphology" returned no directly comparable studies. Barreiro et al. (1997) analysed excursion-set correlations for non-Gaussianity but did not use lacunarity. No prior work applying lacunarity to low-ℓ CMB excursion sets was found.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Angular Momentum of Gradient Field
tests.description: The CMB temperature map is filtered to ℓ ≤ 10 and its surface gradient ∇T computed at each pixel. A vector field V = r × ∇T is integrated over the sphere to yield total angular momentum L. The anomaly statistic is |L|. For a real scalar field, |L| = 2√(2π)|Im(a₁,₁)|, so it depends solely on the ℓ=1 dipole mode and measures any net directional circulation in the low-ℓ gradient pattern.
tests.hypothesis: The observed CMB temperature map exhibits an anomalous low-ℓ angular momentum of its gradient field, indicating a statistically significant deviation in the expected Gaussian, isotropic pattern.
tests.justification: The test statistic |L| measures the magnitude of a vector, which is intrinsically non-negative. The described anomaly is explicitly directional—an "unexpected directional preference" or "anomalous dipole alignment"—and the analysis code's interpretation and p-value focus are on whether Planck's value is anomalously *large* compared to simulations. The physics indicates that |L| directly amplifies the imaginary part of the ℓ=1 coefficient, making an unexpectedly high value a clear indicator of a preferred axis or non-Gaussianity. There is no physical motivation to consider an abnormally *low* value as the primary anomaly; thus, a one-tailed (upper tail) test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck |L| = 5.905. Simulation mean ± std = 14.24 ± 10.95. The observed value lies below the simulation mean with z = −0.76 and an upper-tail p-value of 0.735. The anomaly ratio (Planck/sim mean) is 0.415.
tests.interpretation: The Planck angular momentum magnitude is entirely unremarkable—slelow the median expectation from isotropic Gaussian realizations but well within the central 68% of simulations. There is no evidence of an anomalous dipole alignment or directional preference in the low-ℓ temperature gradient field.
tests.literature: The specific angular-momentum-of-gradient statistic does not appear in the searched literature. Related work examines low-ℓ multipole alignments using multipole vectors (Abramo et al. 2006, arXiv:astro-ph/0604361; Das & Souradeep 2012, arXiv:1210.2861), but those test inter-multipole correlations rather than a global circulation integral. Since |L| reduces to a dipole test, it connects loosely to existing dipole-anomaly studies but uses a distinct mathematical construction.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Skewness Scale Dependence Slope
tests.description: For each ℓ_max ∈ {5, 8, 10, 13, 15, 18, 20}, the CMB temperature map is band-limited and pixel skewness γ(ℓ_max) = ⟨T³⟩/⟨T²⟩^(3/2) computed over unmasked pixels. A linear fit of γ versus ℓ_max yields the slope Δγ/Δℓ_max, capturing whether low-ℓ asymmetry strengthens, weakens, or reverses across the large-scale multipole range. The statistic is compared against 1000 ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The slope of pixel skewness versus ℓ_max for low multipoles is different from zero, indicating scale-dependent non-Gaussianity in the CMB temperature map compared to the LCDM prediction.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, designed to detect any deviation from the expected zero slope under a Gaussian LCDM model, without a prior directional prediction. The description and code allow for anomalies that could be positive or negative, reflecting various physical sources (e.g., foregrounds or exotic effects), and the summary function explicitly computes a two-sided p-value. Thus, a two-tailed test is appropriate for assessing whether the slope is statistically different from zero in either direction.
tests.results_text: Planck slope = −0.00163. Simulation mean ± std = −0.00013 ± 0.01510. The z-score is −0.10, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.91. The Planck value lies well within the simulation distribution, near the median.
tests.interpretation: The pixel skewness shows no significant dependence on ℓ_max in the Planck data. The measured slope is entirely consistent with zero, indicating no detectable scale-dependent non-Gaussianity in the temperature distribution across the low-ℓ range. There is no evidence for residual foreground contamination, ISW-lensing coupling, or exotic sources preferentially affecting particular angular scales.
tests.literature: No directly comparable study was found. Existing low-ℓ non-Gaussianity analyses (e.g., Copi et al. 2004 on multipole vectors; Abramo et al. 2006 on alignment tests) focus on phase correlations and multipole geometry rather than pixel-domain skewness as a function of ℓ_max. The search returned no prior work using this specific statistic.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Shannon Entropy
tests.description: Band-limiting the Planck temperature map to ℓ ≤ 30, this test computes the gradient magnitude at each pixel, forms its histogram, and calculates the Shannon entropy as a scalar statistic to measure the complexity/disorder of the large-scale gradient texture.
tests.hypothesis: The Shannon entropy of the gradient magnitude for the low-ℓ CMB temperature map deviates from the characteristic level produced by a statistically isotropic Gaussian LCDM model.
tests.justification: The test description and analysis code are designed to detect any anomaly in the complexity or disorder of the large-scale temperature gradient texture, with no prior directional preference. The code explicitly computes both upper and lower p-values and uses a two-sided p-value for interpretation, indicating that deviations in either higher or lower entropy are considered potentially anomalous. Thus, a two-tailed test is appropriate for this exploratory analysis.
tests.results_text: Planck's observed entropy is 3.9948, compared to a simulation mean ± std of 4.0096 ± 0.0825. The z-score is -0.18, yielding a two-tailed p-value of 0.756.
tests.interpretation: The result shows no significant deviation from the ΛCDM expectation. The low-ℓ gradient texture has an entropy level completely consistent with the statistical isotropy and Gaussianity of the standard model.
tests.literature: This test is a novel combination of gradient fields and information-theoretic entropy. While the analysis of large-angle gradients is known (e.g., Bernui et al., 2014, using gradient vectors), applying Shannon entropy to the gradient magnitude distribution is a distinct approach.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Extreme-Temperature Laplacian Asymmetry
tests.description: This test computes the spherical Laplacian (∇²T) of the CMB temperature map low-pass filtered to ℓ ≤ 20. Pixels with temperatures beyond ±2σ from the mean are classified as hot or cold extremes. The statistic is the natural log of the ratio of the mean squared Laplacian for hot extremes to that for cold extremes.
tests.hypothesis: The CMB temperature map, when low-pass filtered to ℓ ≤ 20, exhibits a significant asymmetry in the mean squared Laplacian (curvature) between extremely hot and extremely cold large-scale regions, indicating an anomaly in the distribution of sharp features relative to standard ΛCDM predictions.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory and designed to detect any deviation in the Laplacian asymmetry statistic from simulations, without a strong prior directional prediction. The code computes both upper and lower p-values and interprets the direction based on the statistic's value (e.g., positive or negative), confirming a two-tailed approach to assess differences in either direction.
tests.results_text: The Planck statistic (ln ratio) is 0.0049. Simulations (ΛCDM) yield a mean ± standard deviation of 0.0048 ± 0.3640. The z-score is 0.00, with a two-tailed p-value of 0.992.
tests.interpretation: The value is consistent with zero asymmetry between the Laplacian (curvature) sharpness of extremely hot and cold large-scale CMB regions, indicating no departure from the standard model prediction.
tests.literature: Tests examining directional or scale-dependent CMB anomalies often use statistics like the dipole or multipole vectors. While the Laplacian is a common local descriptor, the specific use of an extreme-temperature Laplacian asymmetry ratio is not a standard, well-documented statistic in the literature for anomaly detection.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Excursion-Set Boundary Fractal Index
tests.description: Box-counting fractal dimension D of the zero-threshold excursion-set boundary for the CMB temperature map band-limited to ℓ ≤ 30. Grids of side-length 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 pixels are laid over boundary pixels; log N(s) vs log(1/s) is fit to yield D, where D ≈ 1 indicates smooth contours (Gaussian expectation) and D → 2 indicates space-filling, non-Gaussian structure.
tests.hypothesis: The fractal dimension D of the low-ℓ excursion-set boundary deviates from the distribution expected under Gaussian random fields, indicating potential non-Gaussian structure in the CMB temperature map.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, designed to detect anomalies in either direction—D could be lower (smoother contours) or higher (more complex boundaries) than expected from Gaussian simulations. The code calculates both upper and lower p-values and combines them into a two-sided p-value, supporting a two-tailed approach. The description mentions that non-Gaussian features can push D toward 2, but it does not specify a strict directional bias, as Gaussian fields yield D ~ 1, making deviations in either direction noteworthy for anomaly detection.
tests.results_text: Planck D = 1.2307 (moderately smooth contours with some branching). Simulation mean ± std = 1.2254 ± 0.0044 over 1000 Gaussian realisations. z = 1.22, two-sided p = 0.232. The observed value ranks at the 88.4th percentile of simulations—slightly elevated but well within Gaussian expectations.
tests.interpretation: The Planck low-ℓ excursion-set boundary has a fractal dimension consistent with Gaussian random-field predictions. The modest positive offset (z ≈ 1.2) could reflect mild additional branching or roughness, but falls far short of statistical significance. There is no evidence for anomalous filamentary or patchy non-Gaussian geometry at large angular scales.
tests.literature: No prior studies were found applying box-counting fractal dimension of excursion-set boundaries to CMB temperature maps. Existing related work involves Minkowski functionals, genus statistics, lacunarity, and multifractal spectra of CMB fields, which summarise bulk topology or full multifractal spectra rather than the boundary's intrinsic scaling dimension. The search returned no directly comparable CMB analyses.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Excursion Set Isoperimetric Deficit
tests.description: For the Planck CMB temperature map band-limited to ℓ ≤ 20, an excursion set is defined as pixels exceeding the mean (0 µK). The isoperimetric deficit D = 1 − (4πA/P²) is computed from the set's area A and boundary perimeter P, quantifying shape compactness: D = 0 for a perfect hemisphere, approaching 1 for fractal-like boundaries.
tests.hypothesis: The isoperimetric deficit of the low-ℓ hot excursion set in the Planck CMB temperature map deviates significantly from the distribution expected under ΛCDM simulations, indicating an anomaly in the compactness or boundary structure of the hot region.
tests.justification: The test measures compactness, and anomalies could appear as either unusually compact (low deficit) or highly convoluted (high deficit) boundaries. The analysis code computes both upper and lower p-values and a two-sided p-value, showing no inherent directional bias. Since this is an exploratory anomaly test without a prior expectation for a specific change, a two-tailed test is appropriate for detecting deviations in either direction.
tests.results_text: Planck deficit D = 0.9968, indicating an extremely complex, near-fractal boundary structure for the hot excursion region. Simulation mean ± std = 0.9960 ± 0.0007 from 1000 ΛCDM realisations. The Planck value ranks 933/1000. z-score = 1.23, two-sided p-value = 0.134.
tests.interpretation: The hot excursion region in the low-ℓ Planck map exhibits highly convoluted boundary morphology, but this is expected: both Planck data and simulations yield deficits near 0.996, reflecting that the ℓ ≤ 20 excursion set on a sphere inherently has a complex, fragmented boundary. The slight excess (z = 1.23) is not statistically significant. The boundary complexity is a generic geometric property of low-ℓ excursion sets rather than an anomaly.
tests.literature: No prior studies were found applying the isoperimetric deficit specifically to CMB excursion sets. Related work includes excursion set correlation analyses for non-Gaussianity testing (Barreiro et al. 1997) and isoperimetric deficit theory in differential geometry (Röger & Schätzle 2011), but neither addresses this particular statistic on CMB maps.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Interquartile Range
tests.description: This test measures the statistical dispersion of spatial variation in the large-scale (ℓ=2-10) CMB temperature pattern by computing the interquartile range (IQR) of the gradient magnitudes. The gradient is calculated from the low-ℓ spherical harmonic coefficients, providing a robust, non-parametric summary of the map's spatial gradient structure.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ (ℓ=2-10) gradient magnitude interquartile range (IQR) of the CMB temperature map deviates significantly from the distribution expected based on simulations, indicating an anomaly in the spatial variation of large-scale temperature patterns.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory and aims to detect any significant deviation from the simulated distribution, whether the gradient dispersion is unusually low (indicating a smoother low-ℓ pattern) or unusually high (indicating sharper features). This is evident from the code, which computes both upper and lower p-values and uses a two-sided p-value for interpretation, with no directional preference specified in the test description.
tests.results_text: The Planck IQR value is 113.82 µK/rad. This is lower than the simulation mean of 135.47 ± 12.31 µK/rad, corresponding to a z-score of -1.76 and a two-tailed p-value of 0.074.
tests.interpretation: The large-scale CMB temperature map exhibits notably lower gradient dispersion than expected from ΛCDM simulations, indicating a smoother-than-anticipated pattern at the largest angular scales. However, this deviation does not reach the conventional 2σ significance threshold (p < 0.05).
tests.literature: While low-ℓ anomalies like the quadrupole-octupole alignment and hemispherical power asymmetry are well-studied, the gradient magnitude IQR as a statistic appears to be a new probe of large-scale spatial variation. A direct literature comparison for this specific metric was not found (see note below).

tests.title: Low-ℓ Mean Absolute Gaussian Curvature
tests.description: The mean absolute Gaussian curvature ⟨|K|⟩ of the band-limited (ℓ ≤ 30) CMB temperature map is computed at nside=256. For each pixel, first and second partial derivatives of T(θ,φ) are estimated via spherical harmonic relations and finite differences, then K = (T_θθ·T_φφ − T_θφ²)/(1 + T_θ² + T_φ²)² is evaluated. The spatial average of |K| quantifies the intrinsic curvature of the large-scale temperature surface.
tests.hypothesis: The mean absolute Gaussian curvature of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map (Planck) differs from the expected value under Gaussian simulations.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, aiming to detect any anomaly in large-scale temperature structure that could increase or decrease curvature. The code computes both upper and lower p-values, and the description does not specify a predicted direction of deviation.
tests.results_text: Planck ⟨|K|⟩ = 6.59×10². Simulations yield mean ± std = 4.11×10² ± 6.48×10² (n=1000). The Planck value ranks at the 85.4th percentile of simulations (z = 0.38, two-sided p = 0.292).
tests.interpretation: The Planck mean absolute Gaussian curvature is higher than the simulation mean but well within the broad distribution of Gaussian realisations. The large scatter in simulations (std comparable to the mean) reflects sensitivity to sample variance at low ℓ. No evidence for non-Gaussianity or anomalous large-scale curvature structure is found.
tests.literature: No prior studies applying Gaussian curvature of the CMB temperature surface as an anomaly statistic were found in the literature. Related topological and morphological analyses of the CMB (e.g., Minkowski functionals) are well-established but operate on different scalar quantities.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Coefficient of Variation
tests.description: The gradient magnitude of the CMB temperature field is computed via spherical harmonic derivatives up to ℓ_max=10. For each unmasked pixel, the gradient magnitude |∇T| = √((dT/dθ)² + (1/sinθ · dT/dφ)²) is calculated. The coefficient of variation (CoV = std/mean of |∇T|) is then extracted as a single scalar probing the relative spatial variability of low-ℓ temperature gradients, sensitive to anisotropic or non-Gaussian structures.
tests.hypothesis: The coefficient of variation of the low-ℓ gradient magnitude in the Planck CMB map is consistent with the distribution predicted by the standard ΛCDM model (no anomaly in gradient variability).
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect deviations from the ΛCDM expectation in either direction—either unusually high variability (which could indicate anisotropic or non-Gaussian structures) or unusually low variability (which could indicate an overly uniform gradient field). The analysis code explicitly computes both p-values and a two-sided p-value, and the interpretation logic in `summarize_results` checks for both higher and lower variability relative to simulations. Since there is no strong prior prediction for a specific direction of deviation, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck CoV = 0.515; simulation mean ± std = 0.528 ± 0.028. The Planck value corresponds to z = −0.44 and a two-sided p-value of 0.702, placing it at the 35th percentile of 1000 ΛCDM realizations—well within the interquartile range.
tests.interpretation: The gradient variability in Planck data is entirely typical of ΛCDM expectations. No evidence for unusually high variability (which would suggest anisotropic/non-Gaussian structure) or unusually low variability (suggesting an overly uniform gradient field) is found. The CoV lies comfortably within one standard deviation of the simulation mean.
tests.literature: No prior studies using gradient magnitude coefficient of variation as a CMB anomaly statistic were identified in available literature. Related low-ℓ anomaly work—such as multipole vector analyses (Copi et al. 2004) and bipolar power spectrum tests (Ghosh et al. 2007)—addresses statistical isotropy through entirely different statistics. This gradient-based approach probes complementary spatial structure but has no direct published precedent.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Laplacian Kurtosis
tests.description: Band-limit the Planck temperature map to ℓ ≤ 20, construct the Laplacian field ∇²T by multiplying spherical harmonic coefficients by −ℓ(ℓ+1), and compute the excess kurtosis of pixel-space values over unmasked pixels. For a Gaussian CMB, the Laplacian remains Gaussian with zero excess kurtosis; localized features like the Cold Spot produce heavier tails and nonzero kurtosis. The observed value is compared against 1000 ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The observed Planck CMB temperature map has non-zero excess kurtosis in its low-ℓ Laplacian field, indicating non-Gaussian features compared to Gaussian LCDM simulations.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any deviation from Gaussianity, as the test description mentions "nonzero excess kurtosis" without specifying a direction (could be heavier or lighter tails), and the code computes both one-sided p-values and a two-sided p-value for comparison, implying a two-tailed approach for exploring anomalies in either direction.
tests.results_text: Observed excess kurtosis of ∇²T = −0.0711. Simulation mean ± std = 0.0216 ± 0.1573. z-score = −0.59, two-tailed p-value = 0.608. The Planck value ranks at the 30.4th percentile of simulations, well within the interquartile range.
tests.interpretation: The observed kurtosis is entirely consistent with Gaussian ΛCDM expectations. The small negative value indicates very slightly lighter tails than the simulation mean, but at negligible statistical significance. There is no evidence of anomalous curvature from localized non-Gaussian features at low multipoles.
tests.literature: Monteserin et al. (2006) studied the Laplacian among other scalar quantities as a non-Gaussianity detector on CMB maps, including its kurtosis response. However, they used Edgeworth-expanded non-Gaussian simulations rather than testing excess kurtosis of the low-ℓ Laplacian field specifically against Planck data. No directly comparable published result for this exact test was found.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Great Circle Sign-Coherence Ratio
tests.description: For a band-limited temperature map (ℓ_max = 32), 1000 great circles are randomly selected. Along each circle, 360 equidistant temperature samples are drawn and the proportion of consecutive sample pairs sharing the same sign (both positive or both negative) is computed. Averaging over all circles yields a single scalar measuring large-scale spatial coherence of temperature anomalies.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map exhibits a statistically significant higher low-ℓ great circle sign-coherence ratio than expected from a statistically isotropic, Gaussian ΛCDM model, indicating an anomalous large-scale spatial coherence.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states that "a significantly high value would indicate a coherent, possibly dipole-like, large-scale structure," establishing a clear directional expectation. The test's purpose is specifically to detect excessive spatial coherence along great circles, which manifests as a ratio significantly above the baseline expectation of ~0.5 for an isotropic field. Therefore, the analysis should focus on the upper tail of the simulated distribution to identify whether the observed Planck statistic is unusually high.
tests.results_text: Planck sign-coherence ratio = 0.9312, compared to simulation mean ± std = 0.9359 ± 0.0037 (z = −1.29, two-sided p = 0.176). The Planck value ranks at the 8.8th percentile of simulations—below the interquartile range but in the *opposite* direction from the hypothesis, indicating slightly *lower* coherence than typical ΛCDM realizations rather than higher.
tests.interpretation: The Planck low-ℓ temperature map shows no excess sign-coherence along great circles. If anything, it exhibits marginally *more* rapid sign changes than typical simulations, though not at a statistically significant level. The result provides no support for a coherent, dipole-like large-scale structure at low multipoles.
tests.literature: No prior studies specifically measuring great-circle sign-coherence ratios were found in the literature. Related probes of low-ℓ anomalies include multipole alignment, planarity, and hemispherical asymmetry (e.g., Gurzadyan et al. 2003), but none employ this sign-persistence statistic directly. The search yielded no directly comparable results.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Mean Hessian Anisotropy Index
tests.description: The CMB temperature map is smoothed to ℓ_max = 32. At each unmasked pixel, the covariant Hessian matrix of the temperature field is computed via spherical harmonic transforms and diagonalised to obtain two eigenvalues (principal curvatures). A per-pixel anisotropy index A = | |λ₁| − |λ₂| | / (|λ₁| + |λ₂| + ε) quantifies directional bias in local curvature. The test statistic is the sky-averaged A, measuring mean local curvature anisotropy in the low-ℓ map.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 32) CMB temperature map exhibits a mean Hessian anisotropy index that is significantly different from the standard ΛCDM expectation, indicating an anomalous degree of local curvature anisotropy.
tests.justification: The test is designed to measure the average degree of local curvature anisotropy by comparing the magnitudes of the two principal curvature directions (Hessian eigenvalues). There is no strong theoretical prior predicting the CMB should be specifically more or less anisotropic than ΛCDM; the anomaly could manifest as either more directionally biased curvature (A closer to 1) or more isotropic curvature (A closer to 0). The provided `summarize_results` function explicitly calculates both upper and lower p-values and identifies anomalies on either side of the distribution (e.g., "higher mean... anisotropy" or "lower mean... anisotropy"), confirming a two-tailed approach is intended for general exploration of this statistic.
tests.results_text: Planck mean anisotropy index = 0.4930; simulation mean ± std = 0.4921 ± 0.0042. The Planck value lies at the 59.4th percentile of ΛCDM simulations (rank 594/1000). z-score = 0.22, two-tailed p-value = 0.812.
tests.interpretation: The Planck low-ℓ map exhibits mean Hessian anisotropy fully consistent with ΛCDM expectations. The statistic falls comfortably within the interquartile range of simulations, indicating no preference for either enhanced or suppressed directional bias in local curvature structure.
tests.literature: Previous online searches returned no prior studies employing a Hessian eigenvalue-based anisotropy index on CMB temperature maps. Related work on CMB statistical isotropy (e.g., Ghosh et al. 2007, bipolar power spectrum methods) addresses different observables and techniques. No directly comparable statistic was identified.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Extreme-Temperature Gradient Asymmetry Ratio
tests.description: The CMB temperature map is band-limited to ℓ = 2–16 via spherical harmonic synthesis. Squared gradient magnitudes |∇T|² are computed using spin-1 harmonic derivatives. The hottest and coldest 5% of pixels in the band-limited map are identified, and the statistic R_hot/R_cold is formed from their mean squared gradients. This ratio tests whether extreme-positive and extreme-negative temperature regions have equal gradient sharpness, which is expected under Gaussian random field symmetry.
tests.hypothesis: The ratio of mean squared gradient magnitudes between the hottest 5% and coldest 5% pixels in the low-ℓ CMB temperature map deviates from 1, indicating asymmetry in gradient sharpness between positive and negative temperature extremes.
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect deviations from Gaussian symmetry in either direction (hot extremes having sharper gradients or cold extremes having sharper gradients). The analysis code explicitly calculates a two-sided p-value (p_two), and the interpretation describes asymmetries in both directions. There is no prior directional expectation, making a two-tailed test appropriate for general exploration.
tests.results_text: Planck R_hot/R_cold = 0.693, with simulated mean ± std = 1.068 ± 0.406. The observed ratio falls at the 15.7th percentile of simulations (rank 157/1000), yielding z = −0.92 and a two-tailed p-value of 0.314. Cold extremes show somewhat sharper gradients than hot extremes, but the deviation is not statistically significant.
tests.interpretation: The asymmetry ratio is consistent with Gaussian random field expectations within roughly one standard deviation. While the observed value lies outside the interquartile range of simulations, the p-value is far from the 0.05 threshold, indicating no compelling departure from isotropy in gradient structure between temperature extremes.
tests.literature: A 2022 study by Khan & Saha examined isotropy statistics of CMB hot and cold spot distributions using orientation-matrix methods (arXiv), but addressed spatial uniformity rather than gradient sharpness asymmetry. No directly comparable statistic combining low-ℓ gradient magnitudes with extreme-region comparisons was found in the literature.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Hessian Determinant Skewness
tests.description: Measures the skewness of the determinant of the Hessian matrix of the low-ℓ (ℓ≤32) CMB temperature map smoothed to 5 degrees. This probes asymmetry in local curvature extrema, sensitive to non-Gaussian features in hot/cold spot morphology.
tests.hypothesis: The skewness of the Hessian determinant is zero, indicating a symmetric distribution of curvature extrema consistent with a Gaussian random field.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, aimed at detecting any non-Gaussian features in the low-ℓ temperature map, with no prior directional preference for positive or negative skewness. The analysis code computes both upper and lower p-values and interprets deviations in either direction as anomalies, supporting a two-tailed approach.
tests.results_text: Planck skewness = -13.82. Simulated mean ± std = -16.17 ± 5.78. z-score = 0.41, two-tailed p-value = 0.80.
tests.interpretation: The observed skewness is typical and well within the range expected from Gaussian simulations. The distribution of curvature extrema shows no significant asymmetry, consistent with a Gaussian random field.
tests.literature: A web search for "Hessian determinant skewness CMB temperature map anomaly test" did not return any direct studies on this specific statistic. Related CMB anomaly tests often focus on Minkowski functionals, multipole alignment, or temperature gradients.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Skewness
tests.description: Compute the temperature gradient magnitude map from the SMICA CMB map filtered to ℓ ≤ 30 using spherical harmonic derivatives on a HEALPix grid (nside=256). The gradient magnitude |∇T| = sqrt((dT/dθ)² + ((1/sinθ)dT/dφ)²) is calculated at each pixel. The sample skewness of the gradient magnitude distribution across unmasked pixels serves as the test statistic, probing asymmetry in the intensity of spatial temperature fluctuations to distinguish Gaussian from non-Gaussian features.
tests.hypothesis: The skewness of the gradient magnitude of the low-ℓ CMB temperature map differs from the expected distribution under a Gaussian random field model (i.e., the null hypothesis is that the skewness matches simulations, and the alternative is that it does not).
tests.justification: The test is designed to detect any departure from Gaussianity in the gradient magnitude distribution, whether positive or negative. The code and description explicitly consider both "positive excess skew" and "negative excess skew" as potential anomalies, and the summary statistics compute two-sided p-values. There is no clear directional expectation; the test is exploratory, aiming to identify non-Gaussian features in either direction. Therefore, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
tests.results_text: Planck gradient magnitude skewness = 0.6501. Simulation mean ± std = 0.6443 ± 0.0697. The observed value falls at the 57th percentile of simulations, yielding z = 0.08 and a two-tailed p-value of 0.862. This is entirely consistent with Gaussian random field expectations.
tests.interpretation: Since |∇T| ≥ 0, the gradient magnitude distribution is inherently positively skewed (Rayleigh-like) even for Gaussian fields. The test correctly compares against simulations rather than assuming zero skewness. The Planck data show no discernible departure from the expected distribution—there is no evidence for excess or deficit in sharp gradient features relative to ΛCDM predictions.
tests.literature: Standard CMB non-Gaussianity tests typically employ Minkowski functionals, genus statistics, or temperature-space skewness/kurtosis. Specific tests of gradient magnitude skewness at low multipoles do not appear prominently in published CMB anomaly surveys. This statistic probes a different aspect (gradient field asymmetry) than conventional approaches, though the underlying physics overlaps with existing studies of CMB topology.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Hot-Cold Excursion Set Boundary Asymmetry
tests.description: For a band-limited CMB temperature map (ℓ ≤ 32), excursion sets are computed at the ±1σ thresholds. The perimeter-to-area ratio (boundary edge count / pixel count) is calculated for each set on the HEALPix grid. The statistic is ln(hot P/A ratio / cold P/A ratio), measuring relative boundary complexity between hot and cold extreme-temperature structures.
tests.hypothesis: The observed Planck CMB map shows a significant asymmetry in the boundary complexity (perimeter-to-area ratio) between hot and cold excursion sets at low multipoles, deviating from the symmetry expected in a Gaussian random field.
tests.justification: The test statistic is a log ratio that can be positive (hot rougher) or negative (cold rougher). The analysis code explicitly computes both upper and lower p-values and combines them into a two-sided p-value. The test is exploratory, looking for any asymmetry without a prior directional preference, making a two-tailed approach appropriate.
tests.results_text: The Planck value of ln(hot/cold P/A) = 0.1020, indicating slightly rougher hot-region boundaries. Simulation mean ± std = 0.0004 ± 0.1164, yielding z = 0.87 and a two-tailed p-value = 0.386. The observed value lies well within the simulation distribution (80.7th percentile).
tests.interpretation: The hot and cold excursion set boundaries are comparably complex, fully consistent with the up-down symmetry expected of a Gaussian random field. There is no evidence of non-Gaussian asymmetry between hot and cold low-ℓ structures. The result is unremarkable and carries no tension with ΛCDM expectations.
tests.literature: Prior work has studied excursion set perimeter estimation (Cotsakis et al. 2023, arXiv:2307.12963) and hot/cold spot isotropy separately (Khan & Saha 2022, arXiv:2211.02929). Direct searches for the specific perimeter-to-area hot-cold log-ratio statistic did not return prior studies; related analyses of hot-cold morphological asymmetry and boundary complexity on the CMB sphere appear sparse.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Critical-Point Saddle-to-Extrema Ratio
tests.description: The Planck temperature map is band-limited to ℓ ≤ 20. Each unmasked HEALPix pixel (nside=256) is classified as a local maximum, minimum, or saddle point based on its temperature relative to its 8 nearest neighbours. Saddle points are identified by exactly two sign changes in temperature differences around the neighbour ring. The statistic R_sad = N_sad/(N_max + N_min) is computed and compared against 1,000 ΛCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: The low-ℓ temperature field of the CMB has a saddle-to-extrema ratio (R_sad) that deviates from the value of 2 expected for a Gaussian random field.
tests.justification: The test is exploratory, seeking any anomaly in the topological structure of the low-ℓ field without a strong prior directional prediction. The Gaussian-field expectation of R_sad ≈ 2 is a baseline, but both higher ratios (indicating more complex, multiply-connected topology) and lower ratios (simpler, isolated peaks/bowls) would be physically interesting and signal anomalous topology. The analysis code computes two-sided p-values and interprets deviations in both directions, confirming a two-tailed approach.
tests.results_text: Planck R_sad = 5654.54. Simulation mean ± std = 5669.82 ± 310.70. z-score = −0.05, two-tailed p = 0.984. The Planck value sits at the 49.2nd percentile of the simulation distribution, well within the interquartile range.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ temperature field's saddle-to-extrema ratio is entirely consistent with ΛCDM Gaussian simulations. The topology of the band-limited field shows no excess or deficit of saddle points relative to extrema, indicating typical Euler-characteristic structure. Note: the absolute value of R_sad (~5655) differs substantially from the continuum theoretical expectation of ≈2, reflecting the discrete pixel-level classification scheme rather than a physical discrepancy.
tests.literature: CMB topology has been extensively studied via Minkowski functionals and Euler characteristic (e.g., Planck 2018 results VII). Critical-point analysis of CMB maps is related but the specific saddle-to-extrema ratio R_sad as a standalone low-ℓ anomaly probe does not appear to have been explicitly reported in prior searches.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Temperature Distribution Excess Kurtosis
tests.description: Band-limit the Planck SMICA map to ℓ ≤ 10 via spherical harmonic transform, apply Galactic mask, and compute sample excess kurtosis κ of unmasked pixel temperatures. Compare observed κ against 1000 masked ΛCDM simulations. Positive κ indicates heavier-than-Gaussian tails (more extreme outliers such as the Cold Spot).
tests.hypothesis: The observed low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 10) CMB temperature distribution from the Planck SMICA map exhibits positive excess kurtosis (κ > 0) compared to the Gaussian expectation, indicating heavier-than-expected tails due to the presence of outliers such as the CMB Cold Spot or other non-Gaussian features.
tests.justification: The test description explicitly states that it is "sensitive to isolated extreme-temperature features such as the Cold Spot." The Cold Spot is a well-known, significant anomaly in the CMB that would manifest as a large cold pixel, pulling the temperature distribution toward heavier tails (positive excess kurtosis). Therefore, there is a clear directional hypothesis that the observed map will show an excess of such outliers, making an upper-tailed test appropriate.
tests.results_text: Observed κ = −0.001, essentially at the Gaussian expectation of 0. Simulation mean ± std = −0.025 ± 0.380. z-score = 0.06, upper-tail p-value = 0.408. The Planck value ranks at the 59th percentile of simulations—well within the central distribution.
tests.interpretation: The low-ℓ temperature distribution is mesokurtic (near-Gaussian), with no evidence for enhanced tails from outliers like the Cold Spot at this multipoles resolution. The statistic is completely typical relative to ΛCDM realizations. No signature of the hypothesized heavy-tailed behavior is detected.
tests.literature: The Planck Collaboration (2020, Planck 2018 results VII: Isotropy and Statistics of the CMB; arXiv:1906.02552) performed extensive non-Gaussianity tests at low multipoles using Minkowski functionals, bispectrum analyses, and temperature distribution moments. Their findings generally showed consistency with Gaussianity at large angular scales, though certain statistics (e.g., local non-Gaussianity from the Cold Spot region) showed mild tension. The present excess kurtosis result is consistent with these broader findings of no significant departure from Gaussianity in the low-ℓ temperature distribution.

tests.title: Low-ℓ Gradient Magnitude Hot-Cold Asymmetry
tests.description: Measures the ratio of mean gradient magnitude (|∇T|) around hot pixels (T > +1σ) versus cold pixels (T < −1σ) in the low-ℓ (ℓ ≤ 32) SMICA temperature map smoothed at 5° FWHM. This tests whether local slopes around temperature maxima are systematically steeper or shallower than around minima, probing potential asymmetries in small-scale structure around extrema.
tests.hypothesis: The gradient magnitude ratio for hot to cold pixels is different from 1.0, indicating asymmetry in local slopes around temperature extrema.
tests.justification: The test statistic is a ratio with a symmetric expectation of 1.0, and the analysis code computes a two-sided p-value, interpreting deviations in both directions (ratio > 1.05 for steeper hot spots, ratio < 0.95 for steeper cold spots). There is no prior directional preference mentioned in the test description; it is framed as a general exploration for asymmetry between hot and cold regions.
tests.results_text: Hot-cold gradient ratio = 1.183 (symmetric expectation = 1.0). Simulation mean ± std = 1.049 ± 0.294. The Planck value ranks at the 70.3rd percentile of simulations, yielding z = 0.46 and two-sided p = 0.594. Hot spots exhibit moderately steeper gradients than cold spots, but this falls well within the interquartile range of ΛCDM realizations.
tests.interpretation: The observed ratio is entirely consistent with Gaussian simulations. No significant asymmetry is detected between gradient magnitudes around temperature maxima and minima. The slight preference for steeper hot-spot gradients is a statistical fluctuation with no evidential weight.
tests.literature: Prior CMB anomaly studies at low multipoles have focused on power deficit, quadrupole–octupole alignment, parity asymmetry (e.g., Creswell & Naselsky 2021, arXiv:2102.13166), and the Cold Spot. No directly comparable gradient-magnitude hot-cold asymmetry test was identified in the literature; existing work examines related but distinct asymmetry measures.

tests.title: Masked Low-l Parity Log-Ratio
tests.description: Monopole/dipole-subtracted SMICA and LCDM masked temperature maps are converted to masked pseudo-spectra for 2≤ℓ≤30; the statistic is S=ln(Podd/Peven), with positive S testing an odd-multipole excess.
tests.hypothesis: The SMICA CMB map has an anomalously large low-\(\ell\) odd-parity excess relative to even-parity power, i.e. \(S=\ln(P_{\rm odd}/P_{\rm even})\) is higher than expected under LCDM simulations.
tests.results_text: SMICA gives S=0.2057. Simulations have mean -0.0165, median -0.0103 and standard deviation 0.1231. The SMICA value is at the 96.5th percentile, with 35/1000 simulations at least as high; the upper-tail p-value is 0.035, about 1.81σ.
tests.interpretation: The data show a high odd/even low-ℓ power ratio relative to LCDM simulations. This is suggestive and passes a 5% one-tailed criterion, but it is below a 2σ-strength anomaly and uses only 1000 simulations.
tests.literature: Low-ℓ parity asymmetry is a known CMB anomaly theme in WMAP/Planck isotropy analyses, e.g. Planck Collaboration, A&A 571, A23 (2014) and A&A 641, A7 (2020), but no directly matching study of this exact masked pseudo-spectrum log-ratio implementation was identified in the provided/prior searches.

tests.title: Low-l Hemispherical Variance Max Contrast
tests.description: Scans antipodal hemispheres on the masked, monopole/dipole-subtracted Planck SMICA temperature map after retaining 2<=ell<=64, computing S=max |ln(V_plus/V_minus)| from hemisphere variances, and compares the same statistic with 1000 LCDM simulations.
tests.hypothesis: Planck/SMICA has an unusually large low-ell hemispherical variance contrast, quantified by a high value of S = max |ln(V_plus / V_minus)|, compared with LCDM simulations.
tests.results_text: Planck gives S=0.408, equivalent to a larger/smaller hemispherical variance ratio of 1.50. Simulations have mean 0.315, standard deviation 0.094, and median 0.302. The upper-tail p-value is 0.162, with Planck at about the 83.8th percentile and z≈0.99.
tests.interpretation: The Planck value is above the simulation mean but well within the LCDM distribution; 162/1000 simulations have an equal or larger maximum variance contrast. It does not reach the 5% anomaly threshold.
tests.literature: Hemispherical power/variance asymmetry is well documented, with local-variance analyses reporting stronger anomalies, e.g. Akrami et al. (2014) found no larger Planck asymmetry among 1000 FFP simulations, at >=3.3σ, and Adhikari (2014) reported large-scale asymmetry at >=3.3σ. This implementation differs by using a direct full-hemisphere maximum log-variance ratio over 2<=ell<=64, and finds a much weaker result.

tests.title: Quadrupole-Octopole Axis Alignment
tests.description: Planck and 1000 isotropic simulations were analysed with the same masked-sky least-squares low-l harmonic fit after monopole/dipole removal. The statistic was S = |n2 · n3|, where n2 and n3 maximize angular-momentum dispersion for the quadrupole and octopole; larger S means stronger alignment.
tests.hypothesis: The observed CMB quadrupole and octopole preferred axes are more strongly aligned than expected from isotropic simulations, i.e. Planck has unusually large S = |n2 · n3|.
tests.results_text: Planck gave S = 0.290, corresponding to an angle of 73.1 deg. Simulations had mean S = 0.530, median 0.540, and standard deviation 0.294. The upper-tail p-value for stronger alignment was 0.744, with Planck at the 25.6th percentile rather than in the high-alignment tail.
tests.interpretation: The observed axes are less aligned than a typical simulation and are far from the anomalous upper tail. This test therefore gives no support for unusually strong Planck quadrupole-octopole alignment under this implementation.
tests.literature: The supplied search results did not identify a closely matching masked-sky least-squares implementation. However, quadrupole-octopole alignment itself is a well-known CMB anomaly statistic, discussed for example by de Oliveira-Costa et al. 2004, Schwarz et al. 2004, Copi et al. 2010, and Planck isotropy analyses. Unlike many reported full-sky low-l alignment claims, this result finds no high-alignment anomaly.

tests.title: Fixed-Scale SMHW Cold-Spot Extremum
tests.description: Planck and 1000 LCDM simulations were monopole/dipole-subtracted on the unmasked sky, filtered once with an R=5° spherical Mexican-hat wavelet, restricted to pixels with ≥90% effective mask support, standardized over valid pixels, and summarized by the minimum standardized coefficient.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB map has an anomalously cold fixed-scale R=5° SMHW spot, meaning its minimum standardized wavelet coefficient is more negative than expected from LCDM simulations.
tests.results_text: Planck statistic = -4.595; simulations have mean ± std = -4.692 ± 0.261. Since more negative values are colder, Planck is not extreme: 61.2% of simulations are as cold or colder, giving one-tailed lower p = 0.612 and only ~0.37 simulation-standard-deviations on the warm side of the mean.
tests.interpretation: This fixed-scale extremum does not support an anomalously cold Planck SMHW spot; the measured Planck value lies well inside the LCDM simulation distribution and far from the 5% cold tail.
tests.literature: SMHW Cold Spot anomalies are well documented: Cruz et al. reported only ~0.2% of Gaussian simulations with such a WMAP cold spot (arXiv:astro-ph/0405341), and Cruz et al. 2006 found wavelet-space significance below 1%, including 0.69% for kurtosis (arXiv:astro-ph/0601427). This test differs by using a single fixed 5° scale and a minimum standardized coefficient, yielding no anomaly.

tests.title: Masked Large-Angle Correlation S1/2
tests.description: Masked Planck and ΛCDM simulation temperature maps were monopole/dipole-subtracted on the unmasked sky, converted to masked pseudo-C_l for 2≤l≤64, and integrated as S_{1/2}=∫_{-1}^{1/2} C(θ)^2 dcosθ. Low values test for deficient correlations above 60°.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck masked temperature map has an anomalously low large-angle correlation statistic S1/2 compared with ΛCDM simulations, indicating a deficit of correlations at angles above 60 degrees.
tests.results_text: Planck S_{1/2}=1320.97 μK^4 versus simulation mean 36187.98 μK^4 and median 18176.00 μK^4. Only 6/1000 simulations were lower, giving one-tailed p=0.006, empirical plus-one p≈0.00699, and σ≈−2.51.
tests.interpretation: Planck lies in the lowest ≈1% of the ΛCDM simulation distribution for this masked estimator, indicating a statistically significant deficit of large-angle temperature correlations.
tests.literature: This is consistent with the long-reported low large-angle CMB correlation / S_{1/2} anomaly in WMAP and Planck analyses. Planck 2018 “Isotropy and Statistics of the CMB” confirms persistence of large-scale temperature anomalies including lack of large-angle correlation; see Planck Collaboration VII, arXiv:1906.02552.

tests.title: Masked Octopole Planarity Dispersion
tests.description: Monopole and dipole are fitted and subtracted on the unmasked sky, low-l modes 2<=l<=8 are estimated by masked least-squares fitting, and the fitted octopole is tested with D_3^max, the maximum angular-momentum-dispersion planarity statistic over axes.
tests.hypothesis: The SMICA CMB map has an anomalously planar octopole, meaning its masked fitted octopole statistic D_3^max is unusually large compared with LCDM simulations.
tests.results_text: SMICA gives D_3^max=0.9465, above the LCDM simulation mean 0.8097 with std 0.1064. It lies at about the 91.2nd percentile; 88/1000 simulations are more or equally planar, giving an upper-tail p=0.088 and sigma=1.35.
tests.interpretation: The octopole is relatively planar, but not significant at the 5% level or at ~2 sigma. The result is suggestive only in a weak, marginal sense.
tests.literature: Octopole planarity and angular-momentum-dispersion tests are well documented in CMB anomaly studies, but the exact masked, monopole/dipole-subtracted, l=2..8 least-squares fitted implementation was not found in the supplied/previous literature searches.

tests.title: Fixed-Scale Local-Variance Dipole Amplitude
tests.description: Fits the dipole amplitude of an 8-degree local-variance field from masked SMICA and LCDM maps after monopole/dipole removal, cap masking cuts, variance normalization, and cap-fraction weighting.
tests.hypothesis: The Planck CMB temperature map has an anomalously large fixed-scale 8-degree local-variance dipole amplitude compared with LCDM simulations, indicating stronger hemispherical variance asymmetry than expected.
tests.results_text: Planck gives amplitude 0.0557 versus LCDM mean 0.0205 and standard deviation 0.0088 from 1000 simulations. Only 1 simulation exceeds Planck; upper-tail p = 0.001, equivalent to 3.09 sigma.
tests.interpretation: The Planck map has an unusually large fixed-scale local-variance dipole, indicating a stronger hemispherical variance asymmetry than expected in these LCDM simulations. The result is statistically anomalous, with a caveat that the implementation used a common per-map normalization where exact per-centre simulation calibration was unavailable.
tests.literature: Local-variance/dipole hemispherical-asymmetry tests are related to known Planck anomaly analyses, e.g. Akrami et al. 2014 and Planck isotropy papers, but no directly matching fixed 8-degree, weighted, per-centre-normalized implementation was identified in the supplied searches.
